body:
I am afraid I still do not understand how is this supposed to work. Where is `__useRenderToTexture` set to true?

The fix only changes `setRenderTargetTextures` from setting `__useRenderToTexture` to `false` instead of `useRenderToTexture`. The value is never set to true, at least I cannot find any such place.
author: OndrejSpanel

body:
Note: `useRenderToTexture` was set to `true` in `WebGLMultisampleRenderTarget`, which was been removed recently (#23455).

What is the functionality this should implement? How can it be tested the functionality works as intended?
author: OndrejSpanel

body:
The flag is only relevant in context of `WEBGL_multisampled_render_to_texture`. 

When the extensions is available, a separate code path is taken which does not require an explicit resolve of multisample data. The function `useMultisampledRTT()` detects this.

However, there is an [edge case](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/f9c68e393a4176e18be6fe0b0a00192f3652c63a/src/renderers/WebGLRenderer.js#L1816-L1823) when `useMultisampledRTT()` should return `false` although mutlisampling should be used and `WEBGL_multisampled_render_to_texture` is available. In this case, `__useRenderToTexture` is set to `false`. It does not matter if the flag never holds `true` or any other value because the check is `renderTargetProperties.__useRenderToTexture !== false`.

author: Mugen87

body:
> I did not add support for refraction mapping in WebGLCubeUVMaps since I thought CubeUVRefractionMapping isn't wanted anymore, see https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/19611. It's physically more correct to model refraction via transmission properties.

This means that mapping type `refraction` has no longer an effect for PBR materials?  So the following visualization is only possible with non PBR materials (like `MeshPhongMaterial` / `MeshLambertMaterial`)? I have not achieved a comparable effect with PBR materials and `transmission` properties.

![refraction](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/29681218/157241772-8d83694c-505b-4eeb-959c-6a44aaeba8bd.png)
see: https://threejs.org/examples/webgl_materials_envmaps.html

> If so, we should remove the refraction option from the material browser's drop down when a PBR material is selected. And also remove CubeUVRefractionMapping.

Sorry, I don't got it. Why is the adjustment only limited to the mapping type `CubeUVRefractionMapping`? As I understand it, all other mappings (`CubeRefractionMapping`, `EquirectangularRefractionMapping`) are also affected (for PBR materials) when `refraction` is no longer supported? (https://threejs.org/docs/#api/en/constants/Textures)

And is the parameter [`refractionRatio`](https://threejs.org/docs/#api/en/materials/MeshStandardMaterial.refractionRatio) for PBR materials still relevant?
author: lavond

body:
> This means that mapping type refraction has no longer an effect for PBR materials?

When `CubeUVRefractionMapping` is removed, yes. The automatic PMREM conversion does already support only reflection.

>  I have not achieved a comparable effect with PBR materials and transmission properties.

The effect of `CubeUVRefractionMapping` is physically not correct so using refraction based on transmission will look different.

> And is the parameter [refractionRatio](https://threejs.org/docs/#api/en/materials/MeshStandardMaterial.refractionRatio) for PBR materials still relevant?

When `CubeUVRefractionMapping` is removed, then no.

>  As I understand it, all other mappings (CubeRefractionMapping, EquirectangularRefractionMapping) are also affected (for PBR materials) when refraction is no longer supported?

PBR materials only supports the cubeUV format. Meaning `CubeRefractionMapping`, `CubeReflectionMapping` as well as `EquirectangularRefractionMapping` and `EquirectangularReflectionMapping` are no valid mappings.

body:
I think that #7071 refers to [BufferGeometry](https://threejs.org/docs/?q=BufferGeometry#api/en/core/BufferGeometry.groups) objects where the geometries were splitted and that bounding sphere refers internally to the same BufferGeometry object. 

In my proposal I was refering to able the possibility of compute bounding boxes into the [Group](https://threejs.org/docs/?q=Group#api/en/objects/Group) object in order to try to easiest the task of frustum culling.
author: eipporko

body: This issue is a duplicate of #7071.
author: Mugen87

body: I really think that is not a duplicate in the way that, as I metioned before, both issues are dealing about different components.
author: eipporko

body:
Oh, you are right. The group terminology actually confused me.

Instead of implementing something in `three.js` have you considered to improve your performance with a separate structure like a BVH?
author: Mugen87

body:
Yep, I was reading in some threads that were metioned another projects like [three-mesh-bvh](https://github.com/gkjohnson/three-mesh-bvh) or [threeoctree](https://github.com/collinhover/threeoctree)

But finally I think that in my personal case, as I have actually structured the scene with some kind of spatial hashing is easy for me perform a pre render process to update the visibility value of the groups outside from the camera frustum and avoid to send all the scene to render.

The reason of my proposal was because meanwhile I was reading the method `projectObject( object, camera, groupOrder, sortObjects )` in the `WebGLRenderer` to understand what was happening, I notice that the method was performing a test for all the meshes presented in the scene. And could be interesting to avoid to test meshes which were grouped as children of a Group with a hierarchically superior bounding box performing outside de camera frustum. If the parent is outside the camera frustum no make sense to test their children.

Was only an idea to discuss if make sense or not, but I think that could improve the general performance. Maybe it has some handycaps or headaches with dynamic groups where the children were performing transformations forcing to the parent to update the bounding box. But with static groups could be interesting.
author: eipporko

body:
If this feature is implemented such that group bounding volumes are static, there will be a lot of complaints and bug reports because the bounding volumes will be quickly considered to be out of date. A dynamic approach requires to keep track of descendants or to recompute the bounding volumes every frame. Both solutions are not ideal.

It's better for now to keep this complexity out of the renderer and manage/sort objects on app level instead.

I've just imported the test asset into `webgl_postprocessing_outline`, updated the camera/control settings and removed the additional basic geometries. It is important however to set `logarithmicDepthBuffer` to `true` when creating the renderer because the asset has such a large scale. Without this, rendering is broken even without using `OutlinePass`.

body: [I posted a PR to fix it.](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/23724) I think it would be better not to use types other than what the class represents because that breaks serialization.
author: sunag
wmcmurray

body: Ohh.. I will check this.
author: sunag

body:
> And that fix was just a workaround anyway, it did not fix the problem [directly where it is](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blame/9d6c37f6acfd62672fd1343b9d7a028142c6af8d/examples/jsm/nodes/materials/NodeMaterial.js#L19).

The behavior is correct here, no extended `Node` or `Material` should use a `type` other than the name of the class that builds it, this breaks any serious serialization system.
author: sunag
wmcmurray
sunag

body:
The `.isSomething` approach should always be used internally then, which reduce the relevance of the `type` property..
is it only used for serialization ? or is there other use for it ?

Because if it's sole purpose is serialization, there is no point in being able to store that the type equals `MySpecialMaterial` inside a serialized format if Three.js can't even unserialize it ! (Three.js does not know where the `MySpecialMaterial` class is, it's part of my application, not part of Three.js, it won't be able to instanciate it).

In other words: If a user want to implement a custom material and also use the serialization mechanism, it **would have to** provide an unserialization mechanism for his custom material, it also make sense to let that user decide what `type` his material should have (because it's his concern, not Three.js's concern). _Separating concerns goes a long way in software architecture._

Besides, just search for `this.type =` inside the `/src` directory of Three.js, you'll notice that pretty much every `type` properties are defined with a manually typed string. It might seem tedious, but when the project has like 85 000 github stars, the priority should be to make sure the codebase is stable for all those users, not saving 10 minutes for one developer, I think :)
author: wmcmurray

body: Sorry, but there seems to be a diversity of opinions here, I wouldn't like to confuse bugs and different types of approach, so if other problems arise in this sense we can review this, I think 85k was achieved because Three.js is always open to improvement...
author: sunag

> It might seem tedious, but when the project has like 85 000 github stars, the priority should be to make sure the codebase is stable for all those users, not saving 10 minutes for one developer, I think :)

Any chance you can explain the problem in a more clear and concise way?
I'm having a hard time reading your posts...
author: mrdoob

body:
That one slipped, sorry ! 🫶 😬 

I guess everything is fine for now, **[his PR](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/24723) probably patched the issue**.

I'm just afraid this issue could come back (a third time) if we are still using `this.constructor.name` to define `this.type` ([here](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/e732f0ca0f19372e1bf0fb12313a7ecaa45b73e3/examples/jsm/nodes/materials/NodeMaterial.js#L19) and [there](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/9d6c37f6acfd62672fd1343b9d7a028142c6af8d/examples/jsm/nodes/core/Node.js#L23-L27)), because it gives an unexpected value when the class is extended.

The solution is yet very simple, we just need to define `this.type` with a manually typed string instead of the dynamic value provided by `this.constructor.name` (like [it's done](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/e732f0ca0f19372e1bf0fb12313a7ecaa45b73e3/src/geometries/BoxGeometry.js#L11) [everywhere](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/e732f0ca0f19372e1bf0fb12313a7ecaa45b73e3/src/core/Object3D.js#L41) [else](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/e732f0ca0f19372e1bf0fb12313a7ecaa45b73e3/src/materials/Material.js#L20) [in the](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/e732f0ca0f19372e1bf0fb12313a7ecaa45b73e3/src/materials/MeshPhysicalMaterial.js#L21) [codebase](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/e732f0ca0f19372e1bf0fb12313a7ecaa45b73e3/src/materials/PointsMaterial.js#L12)).
author: wmcmurray

body: When I did the [new `WebGLNodeBuilder`](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/24453) I must not have synced all the updates, this is one of the "big updates" problems. One of the safest methods of testing Three.js is the examples, and then the unit test that we are yet to implement for the nodes. The reason this has been repeated is the lack of an example that we can test after updates of "high impact" like new `WebGLNodeBuilder`, for that reason I added a[ simple example in this PR](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/24723/files#diff-529a005ecfd1bfd1f80ce19ea14c78b41aced39e2a41796557ebbdf19886c467) to avoid repeating this type of problem.

body:
> Though the result works fine on most of my desktop computers AND on my iPad, it fail with wierd effect on one of my desktops.

Probably a driver issue. Do you mind testing if it works when using `WebGL1Renderer` on your problematic device? The issue is highly related to the new texture-based morph target code path which is WebGL 2 only.
author: Mugen87

body:
Tested with WebGL1Renderer really works. However:
1) I have tried before to remove the texture mapping and use single color, or even MeshBasicMaterial, but none of these worked.
    Changed back to what is was, just WebGL1Renderer, solved the problem.
2) Using WebGL1Renderer, which means I am using now the older version, I may not have some of the new features. I think this need to be notified in the manual regarding the morph targets, so the developer can be prepared, and when this issue will be fixed, I would like to know about it too. 

Thank you very much
author: GaoOufan

body:
> Strange point is that computer is quite NEW, 

Can you please share more information about its hardware (CPU, GPU, GPU driver version)? It was also be helpful to copy the `about:gpu` report from Chrome into this issue.

A few similar issue regarding morph targets were reported in the past but I was not able to find a computer in my immediate surroundings that reproduces the issue.

> Using WebGL1Renderer, which means I am using now the older version

To clarify, you just use WebGL 1 instead of WebGL 2. Most library features should still work especially if the device supports (half) float textures.

body: WebGPU API cannot use the GLSL (only the WGSL).
author: LeviPesin

body: Great.  So attaching a RawShaderMaterial with WGSL is supported?  Is there an example?
author: colabi

body: AFAIK ShaderMaterial and RawShaderMaterial are supported only by the WebGLRenderer. But there exists a much better NodeMaterial system, which allows to write code in JS that will later be compiled to GLSL or WGSL. WebGPURenderer uses it internally, and all of the WebGPU examples in three.js are made using it.
author: LeviPesin

body: I think it's probably safe to say we'll support a way to write plain WGSL without using nodes, similar to ShaderMaterial and RawShaderMaterial for GLSL, eventually? But perhaps that's not supported yet.
author: donmccurdy

body: NodeMaterial also supports plain GLSL/WGSL with the use of FunctionNode.
author: LeviPesin

body:
That's good to know.  Is there an example of that for FunctionNode currently?

I guess the point is that for complex code, encoding complex algorithms into a node system becomes highly problematic.  When that's coupled with trying to coordinate this behaviour with another system, that becomes circuitous.  The benefit of a system like WebGPU (Metal/Vulkan/DX12) is to expand the complexity of the algorithms and use of more generalized library code.  Having that functionality available in some form would be really necessary
author: colabi

body:
> That's good to know. Is there an example of that for FunctionNode currently?

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/dev/examples/webgpu_materials.html uses it.

> I guess the point is that for complex code, encoding complex algorithms into a node system becomes highly problematic. When that's coupled with trying to coordinate this behaviour with another system, that becomes circuitous. The benefit of a system like WebGPU (Metal/Vulkan/DX12) is to expand the complexity of the algorithms and use of more generalized library code. Having that functionality available in some form would be really necessary

One of the main benefits of the nodes system is the ability to write a complex algorithm that can be used both by WebGL and WebGPU.
author: LeviPesin

body: Great thank you.  I'll look at that example (I had but probably was lost for the other nodes).  I think the node system is great for many cases.  I'm in particular looking at this as a friendly wrapper around heavy WebGPU efficiency exploitations for larger systems, so the cases would be such that WebGL is already past its practical usage case.  Thanks again.

author: LeviPesin

@LeviPesin 

Could one not use those if they desired so? Sounds like ShaderMaterial could be desired over a single FunctionNode.
author: pailhead

body:
> I personally would like to have a single abstraction which encapsulates for WebGPU/WGSL what ShaderMaterial currently does for GLSL.

I don't know how productive this would be, for example setting `@location()` manually? 
But in my view the bigger problem is still another one, a node material system will make the entire shader system pluggable, we will have optimizers and validators on top of 3JS so that is creates the best shader possible considering the renderer, material, scene settings and between the binding of the nodes, these are things that we are a long way or hard to do from achieving by just using `ShaderMaterial`.

> Sounds like ShaderMaterial could be desired over a single FunctionNode.

It would be the ideal way to work with a specific language in a `NodeMaterial` system, intuitively we were already making the function arguments be the inputs of the `varys` in `NodeMaterial`, `WGSL` follows exactly this design. I think that we can add support for multiple `WGSL` functions in a single `FunctionNode` to make it easier to create complex codes.

Indeed. Hence, I think we should not make the mistake to retain problematic development patters but guide users to new, improved material development.

author: tangobravo

body:
Also note, even the OpenGL ES 3.0 spec allows implementations to perform the sRGB conversion after interpolation. 3.8.16 of https://www.khronos.org/registry/OpenGL/specs/es/3.0/es_spec_3.0.pdf contains this:
> Ideally, implementations should perform this color conversion on each sample prior to filtering but implementations are allowed to perform this conversion after filtering (though this post-filtering approach is inferior to converting from sRGB prior to filtering).

So the inline shader sRGB decode is equivalent to something that is still spec-compliant for WebGL2. Using the explicit texture formats gives performance improvements and likely the more correct pre-filtering conversion.
author: tangobravo

body:
The inline sRGB decode produces wrong colors, see https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/22483. The implementation was hacky (compared to `SRGB8_ALPHA8`) and only worked for built-in textures.

TBH, I would rather recommend to remove WebGL 1 support than bringing back the sRGB inline decode. If users don't want the current policy, they have to stick at `r136`.
author: Mugen87

body:
> The inline sRGB decode produces wrong colors, see #22483. The implementation was hacky (compared to `SRGB8_ALPHA8`) and only worked for built-in textures.

As @donmccurdy [mentioned](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/22483#issuecomment-911835712) in that issue - that example is designed to make this obvious, as it tests 0.5 interpolation between two vastly different pixel values. It really overstates the severity of the issue in general, and as I mention above it's even spec-compliant to do the conversion post-filtering.

In practice if those intermediate values between two pixels are important then just increasing texture resolution will reduce the error introduced by post-filtering conversion. The precision errors introduced in the CPU fallback are likely to have a much more severe real-world impact.

> TBH, I would rather recommend to remove WebGL 1 support than bringing back the sRGB inline decode. If users don't want the current policy, they have to stick at `r136`.

It seems the settled opinion from #22689 that it's too soon to remove WebGL1 support. I'm not suggesting removing use of SRGB8_ALPHA8 or EXT_sRGB where available, but just that the inline shader decode is a much better WebGL1 fallback than CPU-side lossy 8-bit conversion.

If the view of the project is that from r136 the WebGL1 output will be regressed in order to offer code simplifications for WebGL2, and that going forward more divergence can and should be expected, that's certainly a reasonable policy but it is one that would involve us sticking at r136 for our projects until we're prepared to give up on WebGL1.

[There are certain ecosystem issue with react-three-fiber packages that bump peer dependencies in patch versions leading to npm install usually requiring the latest three - it's not a three.js issue, but does bring practical challenges to trying to stick to an early version right now.]
author: tangobravo

body:
> WebGL1 output will be regressed in order to offer code simplifications for WebGL2, and that going forward more divergence can and should be expected

I feel this is the status quo. Besides the PR discussed in this issue, #23060 makes HDR not usable on devices without `OES_texture_float` support.

I think it'd be benefial that we settle our attitude on WebGL1 support. That is to say, we either formally announce that "three.js is gradually dropping support for WebGL1", or reconsider relevant changes. This way people will be less suprised when they find something not working on WebGL1 devices.
author: chubei-oppen

body: Here's a fiddle to demonstrate the precision loss introduced by the CPU fallback: https://jsfiddle.net/z1kxn5pj/81/ There's a 2D canvas on top that renders a radial gradient. The three.js canvas is below, with renderer.outputEncoding set to sRGBEncoding. The texture on the left is set as sRGBEncoding too, and looks good. The one on the right goes through the ImageUtils.sRGBToLinear fallback and is then set to LinearEncoding. You can see banding is apparent in the darker areas due to the precision loss. @Mugen87 from your [comment in #22483](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/22483#issuecomment-911465597) it sounds like your expectation (at that time at least) was that the sRGB formats do something similar to the CPU fallback, where they "decode once". To be clear, that is not what the extensions do - they don't change the underlying bytes, but just change how those bytes are interpreted when sampling from the texture. sRGB maintains more precision in darker areas than a linear 8-bit encoding, and hence doing this conversion on the CPU (still to an 8-bit texture) leads to information loss. Converting to 16-bit or float would maintain sufficient precision but cost more memory. One more thing to clarify - the "wrong colors" you refer to from #22483 are only when a sample is interpolated between pixels - sampling on pixels (THREE.NearestFilter) will give identical ("correct") results with the inline decode. The interpolated results with other filters will differ slightly, but the interpolation will still give a smooth, monotonic, transition between the exactly-correct values at pixel-aligned samples. Outside of the conformance test specifically designed to make this obvious, are you aware of any real-world situations where this was a problem?
author: tangobravo

body:
@tangobravo We have encountered a use case where sRGB formats make a big difference. This happens when you use gpu generated mipmaps with sRGB textures. Apparently, under WebGL2 sRGB textures are filtered in linear space and stored in sRGB, while non-sRGB textures are filtered as is, which is in sRGB space if you stored the same pixel values in the image. At high mipmap levels the difference is very obvious (because the filtering is done repeatedly). We have [a simple example](https://github.com/oppenfuture/three.js/blob/r137-dev/examples/screenshots/webgl_srgb_textures.jpg) to show the case.

This gives another reason to allow users to control whether to use sRGB textures, in case they want to deliver consistent rendering in WebGL1 and WebGL2 environments.
author: chubei-oppen

body:
> @tangobravo We have encountered a use case where sRGB formats make a big difference. This happens when you use gpu generated mipmaps with sRGB textures.

Thanks - indeed mipmap filtering is definitely one area where I agree this will make a difference. EXT_sRGB explicitly disallows generateMipmaps() to "avoid" the issue. I believe ANGLE has code for both CPU and GPU sRGB-correct mipmap generation, so even though generateMipmaps() can't be used it would still be possible to have a "correct" fallback for WebGL 1 too.

For me that's a separate sRGB-correctness issue than the post-interpolation conversion; it's that one specifically where I was wondering if anyone had seen any real-world impact.

@chubei-oppen - thanks for your link to the other discussion, I wasn't aware of the WeChat mini program limitations before. In the western world, iOS <15 is the main webgl 1 target (with otherwise capable and modern hardware), but I believe they do expose EXT_sRGB and the floating-point extensions too.

So I was going to suggest perhaps explicitly treating those extensions as required and dropping support for other WebGL1 implementations, but WeChat mini programs as you mention does have a huge audience.

author: makc

body:
> > [There are certain ecosystem issue with react-three-fiber packages that bump peer dependencies in patch versions leading to npm install usually requiring the latest three - it's not a three.js issue, but does bring practical challenges to trying to stick to an early version right now.]
> 
> npm: https://docs.npmjs.com/cli/v8/configuring-npm/package-json#overrides
> 
> yarn: https://classic.yarnpkg.com/en/docs/selective-version-resolutions/
> 
> did any of these not work for you @tangobravo

I don't want to go too off-topic here, but to provide a bit more context:

Our packages are libraries with three as a peer dependency. There was a peer dependency conflict with three-stdlib due to [this issue](https://github.com/pmndrs/three-stdlib/issues/143). Our users could potentially have resolved this through package overrides but often they're pretty new to npm, and having to document that and communicate it would have been painful. Thankfully three-stdlib have managed to relax their requirements in the peer dependency again in another patch release, so that should have resolved things for now.
author: tangobravo

body:
On the main topic of this issue, I can definitely see the arguments for removing the inline decode and agree it's a cleaner and more universal approach to use the texture formats for this. Also after further investigation it seems most of the iOS 15.4 WebGL regressions were in antialiased contexts and affected both WebGL 1 and 2, so it seems WebGL 2 is potentially usable on iOS (just need to avoid antialiased contexts on 15.4).

One remaining practical concern I have even for WebGL 2 contexts is around video - we use a lot of video in our experiences (both from getUserMedia and video files). I haven't tested, but it wouldn't surprise me if many browsers only have a hardware fast path implemented for RGBA formats. I think right now three still assumes RGBA for video and does an inline decode, but the plan is to move that to sRGB internal formats too at some point in future?

For WebGL1 there are now two fallbacks but each with their own drawbacks - on devices with EXT_SRGB you'll get correct texture sampling but no mipmaps, and on devices without the extension you'll get banding in your textures but mipmaps still supported.
author: tangobravo

body:
> I think right now three still assumes RGBA for video and does an inline decode, but the plan is to move that to sRGB internal formats too at some point in future?

Yes. Right now, video texture still use inline decodes and also `texImage2D()` instead of `texStorage2D()`. Browser issues and problems with determining the dimensions of a video texture prevent a migration so far (see https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/23109#issuecomment-1004640157). But I guess when we dump WebGL 1 and the related fallbacks and code paths, we are going to reconsider this issue.

In any event, #23129 is not going to be reverted so this issue can be closed.
author: Mugen87

body:
With https://crbug.com/1256340 fixed, does anything block us from removing the DECODE_VIDEO_TEXTURE define blocks and using hardware sRGB decoding for video instead?

author: Mugen87

body:
I don't think we should start arguing what example modules should be in core or not. We had this discussion in the past and it was just not possible to find a solution that makes everybody happy since the relevance of example files massively varies from user to user. 

Besides, since more and more devs work with bundlers and the `npm` package, this topic has somewhat lost relevance.

In order to keep the UMD build files small, I would not start moving files around. And to be honest, I would appreciate if we could spend our energy on other, more important issues.
author: Mugen87

body:
> it was just not possible to find a solution that makes everybody happy since the relevance of example files massively varies from user to user.

The same then can be said about the core... E.g. it can be said that many users do not need WebXR-related things and that then they should be moved to examples...

As for me, I understand modules in examples as either they have quite low use, they are not stable enough, or they are almost unmaintained. I think all these three conditions are not true for GLTFLoader, OrbitControls, and BufferGeometryUtils.
author: LeviPesin

body:
The threshold for "three" vs. "three/examples/jsm/..." should probably not be stability or popularity, but:

1. Is it a building block for the three.js library, or key libraries in the ecosystem? 
2. Is it valuable for three.js to offer exactly [one obvious way](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zen_of_Python) to solve the problem?

Personally I don't think these modules satisfy those two goals. They're popular, but not foundational to other things. There are many ways to implement or customize controls, of which OrbitControls is the most popular but not the "best" in any objective way. There are at least two other glTF loaders for three.js, with their own pros/cons.

I could see components related to post-processing or node materials needing to be pulled into the core build at some point, for these reasons. Stability is necessary but not sufficient, and popularity is orthogonal.
author: donmccurdy

body:
> There are many ways to implement or customize controls, of which OrbitControls is the most popular but not the "best" in any objective way.

This only means that three.js should not provide it as _the only controls_, i.e. this means if the OrbitControls would be in the core, there should be also other controls... And it would be hard to decide which should be moved, and which should not... I understand that now, thank you!

> There are at least two other glTF loaders for three.js, with various pros/cons.

Are they so much different in the functionality from the one in examples? If a user is so much needed in one of them instead of the three.js' one, **nothing forces** they to import the three.js' one from the core or from examples - they can import whatever and from whereever they want. But **most** users are using the three.js' built-in one - and for them it would be just more understandable to import it from the core and not the examples.
author: LeviPesin

body:
> ... But most users are using the three.js' built-in one - and for them it would be just more understandable to import it from the core and not the examples.

I'm not sure I agree with this – isn't it also confusing if some official loaders and controls come from one place, others from another? Why should switching from OrbitControls to MapControls change how you import the module?

It is a real source of confusion that we provide both `examples/js` and `examples/jsm`, and CDNs add another layer of complexity. I think Spotify's concept of a [golden path](https://engineering.atspotify.com/2020/08/how-we-use-golden-paths-to-solve-fragmentation-in-our-software-ecosystem/) ("the opinionated and supported path to build something") is a good thing, and we don't really have that for our examples today. To me that is the underlying problem that leads people to want things moved into core, but I'm not sure it's the right solution.

Keeping the core build small also makes it easier to avoid gradually slipping into complicated interdependencies. For example, GLTFLoader's spec/gloss materials are not (and shouldn't be) core three.js materials.
author: donmccurdy

body: Personally, I'm a bit nerdy-annoyed that we passed the 150kb gzipped mark so, if anything, I'm on the lookout for things to move out of core instead 🤓

body:
The problem is that `gl.LUMINANCE` can not be used as an internal format with the new `texStorage2D()` API. Its usage was introduced with `r136`.

Since the fragment shader samples the green channel of the alpha map, I suggest you use the `RGFormat` instead:

https://jsfiddle.net/qxgezwuj/1/

author: itsdouges

body:
>  with RGFormat obviously it needs double the size of the array now so each array item now would be red/green? 

Please keep in mind that `gl.LUMINANCE` is problematic since at least one WebGL implementation (ANGLE) has to expand texture data to four channels to make luminance textures work with Direct 3D. This is not necessary when using `RGFormat` (or `RedFormat`).

> I do get one warning however which is THREE.WebGLTextures: sRGB encoded textures have to use RGBAFormat and UnsignedByteType - is that a benign warning? Everything is working as expected even with this warning.

I suggest you ask this question at the [forum](https://discourse.threejs.org/) or stackoverflow. If it turns out to be a problem of `three.js`, we can reopen this issue.

Most three.js contributors are not dedicated maintainers. With very few exceptions, we are doing this for free on our own time. If you are not comfortable making pull requests that's OK, but please respect our time in addition to your own.

There may be a misunderstanding here — perhaps you're using TypeScript and it's giving warnings and errors in some particular situation? See https://github.com/three-types/three-ts-types if so. But in any case, there is no requirement to use Float32BufferAttribute etc. We can't see these errors, but if they're coming from _three.js itself_ we can fix them. body:
> Also, as I suggested https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/23776#issuecomment-1077562617, WebGPURenderer._clearAlpha should be initialized to zero.

The clear alpha value in `WebGLRenderer` is `1` by default since the canvas is not blended with the HTML background unless you apply `alpha: true` or use `renderer.setClearAlpha()/setClearColor()` with an alpha value < 1. It seems more consistent if `WebGPURenderer` does not behave differently.

body:
`(-1, -1, -1)` produces a reflection in the object matrix which is not supported. Please stick to positive scaling.

What is your use case for reflecting the geometry?
author: Mugen87

body:
Related:

> ...it is possible to compute a choice of translation 𝑇, rotation 𝑅, and scale 𝑆 with the same combined result as [matrix] 𝑀. But there is no guarantee that these will be the same values originally used to create 𝑀, as we'll see below...

https://gamedev.stackexchange.com/questions/117528/calculate-matrix-transformation-components-separately

Matrix decomposition (or higher-level methods like getWorldScale) cannot be guaranteed to return the same translation/rotation/scale (TRS) components as the originals. Repeated round-trip conversions between matrixes and TRS components should be avoided.
author: donmccurdy

body:
Scaling by ( - 1, - 1, 1 ), for example, is equivalent to a rotation-only around the z-axis.

`quaternion` and `scale` are coupled in such cases and you can't consider them individually.
author: WestLangley

body:
> What is your use case for reflecting the geometry?

@Mugen87 I got here because I'm building a scene editor that uses THREE.TransfromControls to change the scale of objects in a scene. It seems that in scale mode, TransformControls will set the scale dimensions to be negative if you shrink an object beyond the point where it disappears. And this is somewhat useful for turning objects inside out.

> Matrix decomposition (or higher-level methods like getWorldScale) cannot be guaranteed to return the same translation/rotation/scale (TRS) components as the originals. Repeated round-trip conversions between matrixes and TRS components should be avoided.

@donmccurdy My scene editor currently works like this:

1) select one or more objects that you want to transform together
2) add all these objects to a new THREE.Group()
3) use THREE.TransformControls on this group to transform the objects in the group as a whole
4) when you're done with your transformations, re-home the selected objects from the Group back to the Scene. Use getWorldPosition(), getWorldScale(), and getWorldQuaternion() to calculate the correct new values relative to the Scene coordinate system.

This is where my round trips are coming from. Essentially, I am using getWorldScale() to calculate the scale my object should have in the Scene coordinate system. Do you think this is a bad approach? It has worked very well so far except for the case of a negative scale.
author: bennlich

body:
If you haven't changed the scale or rotation of the TransformControls group, then I think it would be safe to skip the `getWorldScale()` and `getWorldQuaternion()` steps to preserve the original values. But once you start the whole round-trip it's going to be very hard to preserve that specific _representation_ of the original transform.

Some clever math probably exists that might help to restore a TRS representation similar (e.g. in terms of component signs) to the original TRS, but that's beyond the scope of the THREE.Object3D interface I think, it might be worth pursuing on https://discourse.threejs.org/ or even https://math.stackexchange.com/questions/tagged/linear-algebra.

body:
> The behavier of VTK or OSG to show the same pointcloud is normal.

Both projects are not based on WebGL so it's a bit problematic to use them as a reference. 

Do you see a correct output with another WebGL libraries like BabylonJS or maybe with plain WebGL? If so, we could potentially fix something in `three.js`. 

Sidenote: Next to the driver issues there is another problem. Rendering points with consistent size across all platforms is tricky since the `size` property of `PointsMaterial` is in pixels and devices have different pixel densities. On top of that, the WebGL spec only require a max point size of 1. So there is no guarantee that all devices can handle larger point sizes. If you need points with a size > 1 pixels, one could consider to render for each point a simple plane mesh based on instanced rendering. Each frame, you orient the planes towards the camera similar to sprites/billoards.

body:
Good to hear that! Setting `sizeAttenuation` to `false` and not changing `size` will indeed produce a more consistent result since the point size is always 1 pixel. This size is the best supported one across all devices.

Also notice that in the future with WebGPU points can only be rendered with a size of 1 pixels.

body:
This is a rhino3dm question, not a threejs question. In any case, @leifriksheim has your brep been visualized in a Shaded or Rendered display mode in desktop Rhino? If not, the 3dm file won't have "render meshes" for a brep. From the [doc](https://threejs.org/docs/index.html?q=3dm#examples/en/loaders/3DMLoader):

> Types which are based on BREPs and NURBS surfaces are represented with their "Render Mesh". Render meshes might not be associated with these objects if they have not been displayed in an appropriate display mode in Rhino (i.e. "Shaded", "Rendered", etc), or are created programmatically, for example, via Grasshopper or directly with the rhino3dm library. 

If you are creating the brep outside of Rhino, there is no available method that we provide to convert it to a mesh. You'd need core Rhino for this, either by viewing this in desktop Rhino or calling Rhino.Compute to make a mesh from this brep.

Hope that this clarifies your issue.

body:
I think this is a misunderstanding. 

The default value of `fog` in the abstract material class is indeed `true`. The `fog` property is documented in `MeshNormalMaterial` because the default value is overwritten with `false` to indicate no fog support.

This is actually a bit confusing. It would be probably better to only add a `fog` property to the materials which actually support fog.
author: Mugen87

body: I see, can we instead add Fog support to Normal material? Is there a reason its left out? If not we should handle the crash more gracefully
author: FarazzShaikh

body:
> I see, can we instead add Fog support to Normal material?

The color of a normal material should not be affected by anything (tone mapping, color space conversion or fog).

>  If not we should handle the crash more gracefully

For now, I suggest to update the documentation for `MeshNormalMaterial`, `MeshDepthMaterial` and `MeshDistanceMaterial` and state the `fog` property is read-only and set to `false` to indicate no fog support.
author: Mugen87

body:
Hi.

To export `InstancedMesh` as glTF, glTF [EXT_mesh_gpu_instancing](https://github.com/KhronosGroup/glTF/tree/main/extensions/2.0/Vendor/EXT_mesh_gpu_instancing) extension needs to be used. But Three.js `GLTFExporter` doesn't support the extension by default yet.

But there is an external `GLTFExporter` plugin for `EXT_mesh_gpu_instancing` exntension.

https://github.com/takahirox/three-gltf-extensions/tree/main/exporters/EXT_mesh_gpu_instancing

If you import it and register to your exporter, `InstancedMesh` will be exported with the extension.

The plugin is still at testing phase. When it will be matured and others think it's ok, I may move the plugin into the official Three.js `GLTFExporter` as built-in plugin.

@mrdoob Rather than fixing this issue how about we remove the old code path and only support skinning with float textures? Also see that we did not merge #20704, too.

body:
I could see this being a good addition to [BufferGeometryUtils](https://threejs.org/docs/?q=buffergeometr#examples/en/utils/BufferGeometryUtils), adding a function that takes a snapshot of a Mesh or SkinnedMesh, and returns a new BufferGeometry with morphs and skinning applied. The existing [`computeMorphedAttributes`](https://threejs.org/docs/?q=buffergeometr#examples/en/utils/BufferGeometryUtils.computeMorphedAttributes) utility will get you halfway there.

Depending on how complex that code is (`computeMorphedAttributes` is currently not trivial...) we could possibly invoke it in exporters, but I suspect that might need to be a bit of userland pre-processing.

/cc @gkjohnson possibly relevant to your pathtracing work?
author: donmccurdy

body:
Ahah, that function does look helpful indeed. Why does the code complexity make you think it ought be a user-level pre-process step? The time taken to export?

Requiring a user to perform this as a pre-process step sounds kinda heavy - both in terms of resources and the possibility for users to invoke it incorrectly. I have a scene that exports to a 30MiB OBJ - having to clone that entire scene in order to modify all of the contained mesh data - to use once as I chuck it in to an exporter and then subsequently throw it away seems very wrong to me. The exporter can perform these on a mesh-by-mesh basis, minimizing RAM use.

I suppose there's an argument that if you were doing it every frame you would want to keep the morphed attribute arrays around and re-use the buffers, but the exporter invoking `computeMorphedAttributes` wouldn't preclude a user making an optimisation like that anyway.

The GLTFExporter currently takes a whole bunch of options, OBJExporter could perhaps take an options parameter too, and I should think it'd be possible to figure out in the exporters if it needs doing anyway `requiresMorphedAttributes` for instance?
author: willstott101

body:
@donmccurdy 
> /cc @gkjohnson possibly relevant to your pathtracing work?

The [StaticGeometryGenerator](https://github.com/gkjohnson/three-mesh-bvh/blob/master/src/utils/StaticGeometryGenerator.js) class recently added to three-mesh-bvh ([docs here](https://github.com/gkjohnson/three-mesh-bvh#staticgeometrygenerator)) will generate a new static geometry / mesh based on one or more passed objects with and without deformable geometry. It's designed to update a provided geometry in place to avoid the memory and performance overhead of creating a new object every run. It was originally made to enable the path tracing / BVH libraries to support skinned geometry and morph targets. It supports any morph target / skinned geometry attributes and transforms normals and tangents correctly, as well.

I anticipate maintaining it in that project so I can control performance etc of the function. Maybe some version of it could be added in this project? Though I think it makes more sense to just promote the use of one.

@willstott101 
> Requiring a user to perform this as a pre-process step sounds kinda heavy - both in terms of resources and the possibility for users to invoke it incorrectly. I have a scene that exports to a 30MiB OBJ - having to clone that entire scene in order to modify all of the contained mesh data - to use once as I chuck it in to an exporter and then subsequently throw it away seems very wrong to me.

Any computation required to happen in userland will have to happen in the exporter, as well. In terms of memory overhead - have you run into any issues? If not then this seems like a pre-emptive optimization. I'm sure everyone would like to keep the code in the exporters as simple as possible considering how many of them there are to maintain. An external processing approach is more flexible and will let every exporter just work.
author: gkjohnson

body:
> I anticipate maintaining it in that project so I can control performance etc of the function. Maybe some version of it could be added in this project? Though I think it makes more sense to just promote the use of one.

I'll defer to you here, if it's easier to maintain in a separate repository that is fine!

The performance costs of (1) copying and mutating vertex data and (2) serializing it all to ASCII strings for OBJ or PLY, are going to greatly outweigh the cost of shallow-cloning the wrapping objects. And adding new dependencies to exporters complicates their distribution as well as maintenance — e.g. consumers from `examples/js/exporters` need to import additional files manually. It would be nice to have a short snippet showing how to export a particular frame as a static mesh but I'd lean away from building the option into various exporters directly.

author: Mugen87

body:
`Texture.dispose()` is intended to clean up the WebGL resources allocated by a texture. The problem  with calling `ImageBitmap.close()` is that it invalidates the data source and makes the image bitmap not usable anymore. However, users could create another texture with the same image bitmap at a later point. If the image bitmap has already been closed, the following WebGL warning occurs:

> WebGL: INVALID_VALUE: texSubImage2D: The source data has been detached.

So it's actually better if the image bitmap is managed on app level. Otherwise the engine has to make assumptions which are not necessarily true for all users.

To clarify this topic, we could add a note here in the `Textures` section: https://threejs.org/docs/index.html#manual/en/introduction/How-to-dispose-of-objects
author: Mugen87

body:
Yes, following the same logic because of which `material.dispose()` doesn't dispose of textures, ImageBitmaps should not be closed (although for me it would be ideal to dispose of all the hierarchy with one method call).

I suspect that GLTFLoader didn't use ImageBitmaps before, so this case isn't handled in the viewer 
https://gltf-viewer.donmccurdy.com/
The same happens here https://modelviewer.dev/editor/ (but I haven't read the code in this case, I just see increasing memory consumption).
 
@donmccurdy @elalish I think you should know about this problem. 

Thus, yes, it should be mentioned here:
https://threejs.org/docs/index.html#manual/en/introduction/How-to-dispose-of-objects
And also here: 
https://threejs.org/docs/index.html?q=Loader#examples/en/loaders/GLTFLoader
In the `Textures` section. Because when one creates an ImageBitmap manually it's obvious that it should be closed, but when it's created by the GLTFLoader, it's not obvious at all.
author: deenns

body: @Mugen87 perhaps we could put a `.dispose()` method on the [`Source` class](https://threejs.org/docs/?q=source#api/en/textures/Source), which would dispose of CPU-side data if invoked? Texture.dispose() would not invoke that, but the user could optionally do so.
author: donmccurdy

body:
Um, I'm not sure about that. `dispose()` does normally only free WebGL/GPU related resources. For the moment, it might be more clear if users know they are using `ImageBitmap` and let them handle the disposal on app level.

For the future, we probably have to rethink the design. `ImageBitmap` will be the default in context of image data at some point and a proper resource management by the engine would be desirable.
author: Mugen87

body:
I don't vote to support this use case. It's over-engineering. I would rather suggest to clarify the documentation.

https://threejs.org/docs/index.html#api/en/core/Object3D.userData

The docs already mentioned a limitation about functions. It should be no deal to enhance this section about cyclic references.
author: Mugen87

body:
It's not so much about cyclic references, though cyclic references are fairly unique in the fact that the thrown error just completely prevents cloning... it's more about the constraint on JSON serialization.

(Admittedly modern) standard types like `Set` and `Map` just get serialized to `{}` with `JSON.stringify`.

I agree that the extra option is over-engineering, but `structuredClone` is ECMAScript's native answer to this problem, can using it really be considered so?

I guess it would be a breaking change since functions would throw when cloning, rather than simply being ignored. But if three wants to try and deep-clone `userData` why would we not want to use the better, simpler and less hacky function for it? Is it just cause it's new and we don't want to have code that needs to fallback to JSON-cloning?
author: willstott101

body:
> I agree that the extra option is over-engineering, but structuredClone is ECMAScript's native answer to this problem, can using it really be considered so?

How would you stringify an object using it?
author: LeviPesin

body:
You can't. That does seem like a separate bit of functionality to me though.

Cloning and serialization are separate concerns, evidenced by the fact that Three.js internally has separate hand-written `toJSON` and `copy` functions for pretty much all of it's classes. In fact afaict in the core nothing uses this json hack at all other than cloning userData and `BufferGeometry.groups`.

I'm not certain that `structuredClone` is the perfect solution, but if we we want to continue automatically cloning the `userData` fields without any customizability then I think `structuredClone` is the better solution.

JSON round-trip:
* doesn't support modern container types (Map & Set)
* loses cloned object's prototype reference
* ignores functions completely
* throws for reference loops

structuredClone:
* structuredClone would appear to be a little slower in v8 currently (which surprised me)
* still doesn't keep the prototype reference
* throws when encountering a function

`if (typeof this.userData.clone == "function") other.userData = this.userData.clone()` :shrug: :
* puts the power (of making mistakes) in the hands of users
* kinda confusing and weirdly "magical" - specially named functions seem odd somehow
* could be replicated for toJSON - to have a neat way of putting other THREE.js objects as `userData`
author: willstott101

body: Sorry, but this feature request is out of scope. `userData` fields should only be used for what you can encode to JSON and not with functions or (cyclic) object references. With that policy, `userData` is supported similar to glTF's `extras` field.
author: Mugen87

body: @willstott101  Instead of traversing and removing the cyclical references, why not use Symbols as keys instead? Symbol keys are ignored by JSON.stringify
author: wlinna

body: If it was data I wanted excluded from clone I could just add my own "realUserData" field or something. This ticket is about extended what kind of data is supported in user data by three.js itself. I can store my own data elsewhere if I didn't want three.js' existing clone to work

body:
The problematic line in `GLTFExporter` is:

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/47098e62a5a5706996fc93b446db64720e18cf6f/examples/jsm/exporters/GLTFExporter.js#L1140

It is only used when `embedImages` is set to `false`. Normally, `imageDef.uri` should hold a URL to the image. In your case, a data URI is used instead which is of course not right. The image's `src` property points to a data URI since the texture is imported via `FileReader`. 

`embedImages === false` does also not work if `GLTFLoader` loads the images via `ImageBitmapLoader`. In this case, `imageDef.uri` is `undefined` since image bitmaps do not have a `src` property.

@elalish @donmccurdy Because of the broken character of `embedImages === false`, would it be okay to remove this option from the exporter? I don't see an easy way to fix this for all use cases.
author: Mugen87

body:
So in other words — there's no reliable way to trace a THREE.Texture back to an external URL, but external URLs would be required for `embedImages === false` to work? Scenes loaded from GLBs, or containing KTX2 textures (not to mention files loaded from other formats) would probably be additional edge cases here. Related: #21328.

I think I agree about removing this option, if that's OK with others. /cc @takahirox
author: donmccurdy

body:
> there's no reliable way to trace a THREE.Texture back to an external URL, but external URLs would be required for embedImages === false to work?

Correct!

> Scenes loaded from GLBs, or containing KTX2 textures (not to mention files loaded from other formats) would probably be additional edge cases 

Yes. So the exporter would need to handle image bitmaps, compressed textures and data URIs correctly.
author: Mugen87

body: I'm in favor of removing that option. Single-file GLBs are way more popular anyway, and if someone really wants to separate them they can always use `gltf-pipeline` or similar.
author: elalish

body: Removing the option sounds ok to me because I prefer to make the exporter as simple as possible and, as @elalish mentioned, users may use post processing tools like [gltf-transform](https://github.com/donmccurdy/glTF-Transform). And also they might be able to write the separated texture file plugin with the exporter plugin API? I didn't check in details if the APIs greatly fit to that purpose yet tho.

author: Mugen87

body:
I've assumed that was always the point of `embedImages=false` — the user needs to manage the image hosting themselves and keep track of those URLs. Certainly harder to use than embedded images, but could be useful if you are exporting a bunch of glTF files that share the same textures.

But in any case, it's not clear what we'd really be supporting by keeping this option, so +1 for removing it.
author: donmccurdy

body:
> The problematic line in `GLTFExporter` is:
> 
> https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/47098e62a5a5706996fc93b446db64720e18cf6f/examples/jsm/exporters/GLTFExporter.js#L1140
> 
> It is only used when `embedImages` is set to `false`. Normally, `imageDef.uri` should hold a URL to the image. In your case, a data URI is used instead which is of course not right. The image's `src` property points to a data URI since the texture is imported via `FileReader`.

Makes perfect sense to me, I certainly misused this feature little (semi-intentionally).

What I was really trying to achieve is to have the source textures embedded. With `embedImages` set to `true`, jpegs are saved uncompressed with 4 channels - which is larger than a 3-channel uncompressed png. I don't recall checking if they are actually saved as png.

Relating to this... Is there a proper way to embed the source textures?
author: 852Kerfunkle

body:
Possibly https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/23998 would help you, if the JPEG size increase is new with the r140 release?

But no, GLTFExporter does not have access to original texture data. It tries to export reasonably compact textures at the expense of being a bit lossy, but it doesn't try multiple formats and wouldn't know when PNG could be smaller than JPEG.

If your use case happens to be...

1. load one or more glTF files
2. view and/or edit in three.js
3. export back to glTF

... then [`@gltf-transform/view`](https://github.com/donmccurdy/glTF-Transform-View) would be a lossless (but still experimental) option that preserves the original texture data.

author: 852Kerfunkle

body:
#23592 could help. You can store the original mime type in `Texture.userData` which will be honored by `GLTFExporter`.

> I'm actually on 139.2, but possibly that regression applies to it as well. I've observed a nearly 2.5x increase in size on a very simple model with a jpeg texture assigned.

When you are using `139.2`, you are not affected by the latest regression.

However, since `three.js` does not support RGB formats anymore, `GLTFExporter` was updated to always export PNG (see #23385). This approach produces larger files size however #23592 was introduced to bypass this issue.

Maybe something akin to #23592 can be done - store the original texture in `userData`. Just an idea, I'm certainly happy with the status quo.

body:
@mylesverdon @WestLangley Can you please check if using ANGLES's Metal backend solves the issue? Download Chrome Canary and then use this to startup the app:
```
Applications/Google\ Chrome\ Canary.app/Contents/MacOS/Google\ Chrome\ Canary --user-data-dir=/tmp/c1 --use-angle=metal
```
It seems OpenGL is still the default but soon ANGLE will switch to Metal. Another issue  will be fixed by this migration, too, see https://bugs.chromium.org/p/chromium/issues/detail?id=1329199#c1

author: mylesverdon

body:
Your application code must scale the model to a size appropriate for your camera and scene. By default all glTF models are imported with the defined 1 unit = 1 meter convention, viewers will adjust the camera location and other parameters to center the content from there. [Staging components](https://github.com/pmndrs/drei#staging) for React Three Fiber may also help here.

We can't provide support through github issues, so I'd recommend posting to the [forums](https://discourse.threejs.org/) or Stack Overflow instead.

body:
It's mostly up to the maintainers of the code whether they prefer to work on it here or in another repository, assuming a feature is within the scope of three.js (like post-processing). As a project becomes more complex it may be easier to manage in a dedicated repository with a dedicated release cycle, which is totally fine.

So I'd defer to @gkjohnson or @vanruesc (author of https://github.com/pmndrs/postprocessing), but as they're currently using other repositories I'll assume that's their preference.

body:
Custom materials often copy built-in shader code. If this code still refers to `MAX_BONES`, it will break with `r140` because the define does not exist anymore.

Copy built-in shader chunks is problematic since they can break in any release (whenever we refactor or change GLSL).

body: That error comes from `lodMax` in `PMREMGenerator` not being a whole number. PMREMs are now of a different size. You will get that error if you try to load a PMREM texture exported from an earlier three.js version, for example.
author: WestLangley

body: As far as I can see this is an exchange format used for the oil and gas industries, so I don't think the three.js project will likely build and maintain such a loader. The [source code for all current loaders](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/tree/dev/examples/jsm/loaders) is available as examples of how to write a loader, and PLYLoader or STLLoader might be good starting points. Tools also exist to [convert .rvm files to .gltf](https://github.com/cdyk/rvmparser), that is the format we generally recommend using when possible. Finally, feel free to ask on [the forums](https://discourse.threejs.org/), Stack Overflow, Discord, or Slack if you get stuck while working on a loader or other projects!

body: It looks like Blink has a [default JPEG quality](https://source.chromium.org/chromium/chromium/src/+/main:third_party/blink/renderer/platform/image-encoders/image_encoder.cc;l=85;drc=52f06e6b43ff95eccf79e0a5df8d4d83c029130a;bpv=0;bpt=1) of 92% and [default WebP](https://source.chromium.org/chromium/chromium/src/+/main:third_party/blink/renderer/platform/image-encoders/image_encoder.cc;l=100;drc=52f06e6b43ff95eccf79e0a5df8d4d83c029130a;bpv=0;bpt=1) quality of 80%. And yeah for some reason this default quality is not being used when using `convertToBlob`. I can go ahead and use those defaults for those mimetypes if that makes sense? OffscreenCanvas really is only supported in Blink-powered engines right now and those defaults seem like good defaults for us to use for now.

author: elalish

body:
I remember we've agreed to use a short name for simplicity and flexibility (see https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/17949#issuecomment-969350460). Something like `ImageSource` and `TextureSource` are more descriptive but on the other hand the project's policy was to pick a simple/short alternatives in the past . More descriptive names tend to make the code longer and not necessarily easier to read.

Regarding this policy, I think would prefer to keep the current name.
author: Mugen87

body: It sounds like there was a little back and forth - I think it's possible to have short and descriptive names. `Source` on it's own sounds very ambiguous. It could be an audio source, for example. I was just confused at what it could be when importing it so I made this suggestion. I was looking for something that had "Image" or "Texture" in the name

I think there is a misunderstanding about how planes are defined in 3D space (Hessian normal form). I suggest you always use an instance of `PlaneHelper` to see a visual representation of the (pure mathematical) plane definition.

The clipping plane is where it is supposed to be: https://jsfiddle.net/p7sLgrbk/1/

body:
The project used the term skinning as an alternative for skeletal animation which is not entirely correct. Skinning is something that happens during skeletal animation. [Wiki says](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Skeletal_animation):

> Each bone in the skeleton is associated with some portion of the character's visual representation in a process called skinning

However, all mentioned examples fulfill this definition since they use `SkinnedMesh` and thus a skeletal in combination with skin indices and weights. Hence, I see no need to update the examples with different models.

author: mrdoob

body:
Showing skinning examples that don't actually show any skinning about the same as showing texturing example with 1x1 pixel textures or loading a gLTF with a single triangle. Yes, technically a 1x1 pixel texture would still be showing texturing but it's not an illustrative example of texturing. Nor would loading a 1 triangle gLTF show off loading gLTF files.

In the same way, showing skinning with only 1 influence per vertex is not really showing skinning. It doesn't really matter that it happens to go through the right code path.

I'd be happy to replace the model that that's there with this model above. Would that change be accepted?

body:
Same, I also confirmed @marcofugaro's point this morning, tree-shaking behaves differently when importing from `node_modules`. In the tests I did all the `.prototype.is*` properties cause the class to be included in the final bundle whether you are using it or not, so essentially the entire library is bundled regardless.

For example removing this one line of code makes a difference of nearly 100 KB of files that get bundled.

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/ce66f72b53c51dbb7d1f7f92438061697238afef/src/animation/AnimationMixer.js#L766
author: pschroen

body:
@donmccurdy @marcofugaro to summarize I think we're dealing with at-least three problems in this issue that would need to be addressed:

1. __`Object.defineProperties`__ outside of the classes need to be removed. Inside the class is fine.
2. __`.prototype.is*`__ properties outside of the classes need to be removed. Suggesting the same as @marcofugaro did on Twitter with __`this.is*`__ inside the constructor.
3. __Alternative solution for handling the API changes warnings__, other frameworks or libraries handle this with `@deprecated` decorators and keeping the method warnings inside the classes themselves.

All this to say, in my experience the only true way to have a library fully tree-shakeable is the classes all need to be self-contained, without any side-effect artifacts referencing them, and only imported by other classes that use them.
author: pschroen

body:
> 3. Alternative solution for handling the API changes warnings, other frameworks or libraries handle this with @deprecated decorators and keeping the method warnings inside the classes themselves.

This was discussed in the past, not really feasible and easy to maintain according to the core maintainers.

author: donmccurdy

body:
> ideas in no particular order:
> (a): rewrite Three.Legacy.js to avoid Object.defineProperties
> (b): publish production/development builds
> (c): provide legacy warnings through a separate export

> Ya was expecting that to be the case, that's the trade-off though, more difficult to maintain with messier classes. Perhaps there's some other way we could rewrite Three.Legacy.js?

An option (d): switch to semver, release a version 1.0.0 with all the legacy code removed, and take care of breaking changes in a more standard way going forwards. Few if any other libraries support backwards compatibility in the way that three.js does. Besides problems like this I think it also leads to "ugrade-itis" where users expect they should bump the three.js version number every month and from personal experience I know that's a bad idea and I don't thing it's something we need to, or _should_ encourage. 

I know has been suggested and rejected before, but given that by now it's highly likely most people use three.js via NPM, perhaps it's time to revisit this? There's a standard method for updating NPM packages and three.js is one of the very few libraries that doesn't follow it, since we are releasing a new "minor" version every month. Minor versions are not supposed to contain breaking changes, however any three.js release may contain breaking changes. 

In practice, most three.js releases _don't_  contain breaking changes so the majority of new releases would still be minor or patch releases, we could still stick with the monthly release schedule. The differences would be a) we remove all legacy code and b) we need to consider on release day whether the release will be _major_ (breaking changes), _minor_ (non-breaking changes), or _patch_ (just bug fixes). 

I realize this is somewhat tangential to the current issue although it would solve one of the three problems @pschroen highlighted. If there's interest in discussing it further I'll move it to a new issue.

author: LeviPesin

body:
I suggest to not use this issue for discussions about the release cycle and versioning of `three.js`. Changes in that context are not required to improve tree-shaking.

Regarding https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/24006#issuecomment-1117961200, I think we can solve the first point by just removing all `Object.defineProperties` statements from `Three.Legacy.js`. At all other places in the code base (core and examples), `Object.defineProperties` calls are already inside classes.

The mentioned code in `Three.Legacy.js` can be removed since:
- most of the fallbacks are several years old.
- in general it's recommended to avoid large upgrade steps (e.g. `r70` -> `r140`) but do it in a more incremental fashion instead (e.g. `r70` -> `r80` -> `r90`)
- removing the code would allow us to decrease the file size of the core builds a bit.

For the future, I indeed suggest to keep deprecated properties in the respective classes for a certain period of time (e.g. one year). After that time, they can be removed.
author: Mugen87
pschroen

body:
Adding some more detail here from this morning's troubleshooting, and possibly a fourth problem.

The dead end I hit was on `/*@__PURE__*/` code outside of the classes, most of the code outside the classes don't have the pure annotation, it's not always needed, however I tried adding `/*@__PURE__*/` to test, but some of the code outside the classes are doing stuff, in other words not just declarations.

So current tree-shaking techniques mark them non-pure whether they have the pure annotation or not. Here's just one example of code being bundled when not in use.

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/06160137744f186ad968a95f1eed450e85f7be2f/src/animation/PropertyBinding.js#L2

And adding the pure annotation still doesn't work because the code is actually being used, and that's how tree-shaking works, so it's working properly here.

Input:
```js
const _RESERVED_CHARS_RE = /*@__PURE__*/ '\\[\\]\\.:\\/';
...
const _wordCharOrDot = /*@__PURE__*/ '[^' + _RESERVED_CHARS_RE.replace( '\\.', '' ) + ']';
```

Output:
```js
const _RESERVED_CHARS_RE =  '\\[\\]\\.:\\/';
'[^' + _RESERVED_CHARS_RE.replace( '\\.', '' ) + ']';
```

Note that all these issues are AST parsing of code outside the classes. When tree-shaking is done on an entry point file like `./src/Three.js` there's none of that, the bundler just imports the named classes/objects, and it's done. That's why it works from `./src/Three.js`.

For reference here's the Tree Shaking documentation from Webpack, I used Rollup for my tests but they handle the pure annotations the same, and so far the results seem to be the same across bundlers, so this would appear to be more of a three.js problem with how the code is being output if we are to continue using the flat `three.module.js` bundle as the entry point for the library. 😬

https://webpack.js.org/guides/tree-shaking/
author: pschroen

body:
So I'm going to propose something here that may seem radical to some, but it's not really in 2022, and I believe it's "the path of least resistance" here.

Let me know if you guys agree but I view three.js as serving two use cases, as a three.js "application", and as a three.js class "library". For example I've personally used three.js on non-WebGL projects just to import from the internal math classes, I know I'm not the only one who does this.

- An "application" would need the usual code bootstrap stuff like `REVISION`, `__THREE_DEVTOOLS__`, the check for multiple instances and API warnings.
- A "library" would arguably just need the API warnings.

What I'm proposing here is that three.js finally moves to a real ES module library, that would mean removing all CommonJS and Rollup, no bundling, just the separate classes. Even for the examples, loading them from `./src/Three.js` works fine and not as slow as you may think.

It would require two pretty major changes though:

1. Remove CommonJS support and Rollup flat file bundling completely, pointing the `package.json` entry point and all examples to `./src/Three.js`. Just `"type": "module"`, `"main": "./src/Three.js"`, `"sideEffects": false` is all that's needed.
2. As @Mugen87 suggested move only the most recent method API warnings to their respective classes, and you can still keep `Three.Legacy.js` but just for deprecated classes which will work fine with tree-shaking.

That's it!

I know simpler said than done though right? If you guys are up for it I'm happy to help with this work. 😅

And dare I say, maybe this would be a big enough change to justify a switch to semver as well! 👀
author: pschroen

body:
> ... three.js finally moves to a real ES module library, that would mean removing all CommonJS and Rollup, no bundling, just the separate classes

Throwing out the build feels like a last-resort option to me. Rollup is a popular and well-respected tool, we're squarely in the use case it's made for, and the maintainers clearly don't see [bundles and tree-shaking](https://rollupjs.org/guide/en/#tree-shaking) as incompatible goals. Our builds do much [more than just concatenate](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/dev/utils/build/rollup.config.js) files, all of which would be lost by publishing raw source files.

I'm really hoping we can get a statement from a Rollup or Webpack maintainer about what's going on and how we or they could fix it. If the conclusion is really "bundlers are bad for libraries", then that's a huge deal for the wider JS ecosystem. It's not that we _couldn't_ get by without Rollup, but something feels wrong if it's just three.js coming to the conclusion that this is necessary. 

(I have no opinion on switching to semver, removing Three.legacy.js, or removing CommonJS builds at this time.)
author: donmccurdy

body:
That's fair, and I understand.

Just wanted to put that out there as a pure ES module approach myself and @gordonnl have been doing with our work and used by [OGL](https://github.com/oframe/ogl). It's been possible ever since Node.js added ES module support, and I would argue that's how ES modules were intended to be used, but that's for a different thread. 😅

So if we're aligned on keeping the flat `three.module.js` bundle as the entry point for the library then the path is clear, and we know what to do. The above points all still stand and we can make the bundle tree-shakeable by minimizing the amount of code that's executed outside of the classes.

I do still feel there is merit in changing the entry point to `./src/Three.js`, @Rich-Harris [seems in favour of that](https://twitter.com/Rich_Harris/status/1521650297143132160) as well and avoids all these problems.

He also suggested [agadoo](https://github.com/rich-harris/agadoo) which we should checkout. 🙂
author: pschroen

body:
And speaking of [agadoo](https://github.com/rich-harris/agadoo), spent some time tonight testing it out (nice one @Rich-Harris 🙌), as you pointed out.

> What it doesn't do
Tell you why tree-shaking fails

```sh
Failed to tree-shake build/three.module.js
```

From the `README.md`:

> Avoid transpilers like Babel and Bublé (and if you're using TypeScript, target a modern version of JavaScript)
Export plain functions
Don't create instances of things on initial evaluation — instantiate lazily, when the functions you export are called

I don't like being the guy stirring the pot over all this stuff, though "Export plain functions" would include things like namespaces, so things like `THREE.*` and `MathUtils.*`. I realize this was common practice back in the day, but since ES modules, things have moved towards named imports now, like in the original post that started this conversation by @MarcoGomezGT.

Here's also a good reference on this topic by @bluepnume. 😉

https://bluepnume.medium.com/javascript-tree-shaking-like-a-pro-7bf96e139eb7
author: pschroen

body:
> If the conclusion is really "bundlers are bad for libraries", then that's a huge deal for the wider JS ecosystem. It's not that we couldn't get by without Rollup, but something feels wrong if it's just three.js coming to the conclusion that this is necessary.

Yeah, it seems _much_ more likely that it's something to do with the way three.js is written so we should thoroughly investigate that first (albeit I kinda like @pschroen's radical ideas and feel they warrant consideration, although I don't know right now what side I would come down on). Agadoo seems like a good starting point for the investigation. 

Of the things mentioned here, the only things we do that seem unusual are keeping so much legacy support, and the `.prototype.is*` properties. For the latter, is there another way we can handle this? Modifying prototypes feels kind of archaic when using ES6 classes and the behavior is confusing as [@donmccurdy pointed out on Twitter](https://twitter.com/donrmccurdy/status/1522279662919069696). It would be better to ban any prototype modification in `src/`, if possible. 

Looking through `src/`, beside the `.prototype.is*` and `Three.Legacy.js`, I think prototype modification is only done in the animation system (@bhouston this is a coincidence I swear, nothing to do with our Twitter conversation from this morning 😅). Has anyone tested whether that that gets tree-shaken? 

Regarding the use of `Object.defineProperties` outside of classes, we might need to move the relevant legacy code directly into the classes. Kind of ugly but not technically a problem I think.

Regarding semver, one final point and then I won't mention it again here because I don't want to sidetrack the discussion (even more). The reason I am in favor of switching is because we already do use semver. We publish on NPM and it's not possible to do that without using semver, so we're already using it. We just do so wrongly and should fix that if we can since NPM is presumably where the large majority of devs get three.js from these days.

body:
@mrdoob After merging #24034, `Object.defineProperties()` is only used for two properties now (`vertexTangents` and `gammaFactor`). Do you think we can handle them (and upcoming legacy properties) differently such that Tree-shaking is not affected? 

Would it be an option to move legacy properties and methods to classes and only keep stuff like `export function Font...` in `THREE.Legacy.js`? Now that the project has established a clear policy in context of removing legacy code, it should be more manageable to keep legacy properties and methods at their original spot.
author: Mugen87

body:
> > `instanceof` is even worse for tree-shaking.
> 
> Hm, but why?

With `foo.prototype.isFoo = true` the class can safely be tree-shaken, build tools just aren't perfect at detecting it. But if WebGLRenderer checked `material instanceof PointsMaterial` instead of `material.isPointsMaterial`, we could never tree-shake PointsMaterial because the class is now actually being imported and used.

> We publish on NPM and it's not possible to do that without using semver, so we're already using it. We just do so wrongly...

Aside — We are and we aren't... Semver has a [special case](https://semver.org/#spec-item-4) for versions <1.0.0, where breaking changes are allowed to occur in minor (0.x.0) releases. We're working within that special case. They presumably intended its use for "initial development", whereas we use it for other reasons, but tools like npm and yarn do understand that 0.141.0 → 0.142.0 is a potentially-breaking update.
