author: WestLangley

body:
The warning was added since certain FBX assets use `-1` as a material index. There is no indication in the unofficial FBX spec that such an index is valid nor how it should be interpreted. In any event, `three.js` can't process it since certain parts of the mesh would render with an `undefined` material.

It's probably best to avoid FBX exports in the first place which contain negative integers as material indices. In your case, you might want to switch to glTF if Fusion360 supports it (Blender has built-in glTF support).
author: Mugen87

body:
My experience with this has me thinking that the "lesser of two evils" here would be to continue doing whatever three.js did before, even though perhaps the past decision on how to handle the "-1" material index was not informed by a good FBX spec and may have been, in fact, totally arbitrary. We aren't the world's quickest debuggers here, and trying to root cause and fix these warning messages did flummox both of us. Best outcome here might be to avoid having other people get stumped as we did - better, at least, than people learning by experience not to mix FBX files and three.js.
It might be a good idea to also add a note to the docs to say that three.js support for the FBX format was a "best effort" situation due to the lack of publicly available FBX specs, and suggest/recommend gITF instead.
Or, add a default material to be used if the format contains a "-1" material index, and provide a way for the user of the loader to override the default if they want to.
In our case, regenerating our models in another format is not an easy option to implement as the people that contributed some of them have moved on.

body:
The problem is that we normally want to inform the user when something is wrong with the asset. If we don't log a message, the user could assume "everything is right". 

Besides, the loader actually assign the `0` material index in invalid cases as a fallback. So it's just the question whether we should refactor the warning or not.

How about we rewrite the log so the loader reports it just once? So instead of reporting `Invalid material index` every time an invalid material index was found, we could report the following warning once:
```
The FBX file contains invalid (negative) material indices.
```

body:
> We have no warranty that the world matrices are up-to-date when executing these methods. We need to check if [matrixWorldNeedsUpdate](https://threejs.org/docs/index.html?q=Object3D#api/en/core/Object3D.matrixWorldNeedsUpdate) is set to true before attempting raycasting.

In case there's any confusion here — The conditions under which `.matrixWorldNeedsUpdate === true` are very limited. It just indicates that `.matrix` has been changed since `.matrixWorld` was last updated. `matrixWorldNeedsUpdate` does not tell you whether a parent's transform has changed, or if .position/.rotation/.scale have changed locally. 

If we're talking about recomputing `.matrixWorld` locally when `.matrixWorldNeedsUpdate === true`, I believe that should be OK from a performance perspective?

The other cases are similar causes of confusion. But as @gkjohnson mentions in https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/24727 — the performance cost of the solution could be very high, and we'd need to be quite careful there.
author: donmccurdy

body:
Thanks for the precision, I agree that we need to be careful.

In the general case, the renderer would be recomputing the matrices anyway if there is a need to do so on the frame N+1.

It is what I don't get about your concern on performances, we are computing the same matrices the same number of time but just on the frame N, if we trigger the raycast method. 

Casting on an outdated matrix seems worst to me. For 3D mouse, etc it is fine, it does not change a lot, but on setup you are forced to calculate it manually. 

Please, feel free to enlight me on the subject, I'm still a ThreeJS learner.
author: AxiomeCG

body:
I have no objection to letting the raycaster check `.matrixWorldNeedsUpdate` and updating `.matrixWorld` if the flag is set. No performance problem there, that I can see.

Checking the other conditions (parent transform change, .position/.rotation/.scale change) would be a longer discussion, there are performance challenges involved. Unless that's the proposal here I won't get into it. :)
author: donmccurdy
AxiomeCG
donmccurdy

body:
Sorry, but I think the proposed evaluation of `matrixWorldNeedsUpdate` is no robust solution.

- The flag is a shallow, local signal that is set to `true` when a node's own _local matrix_ is dirtied. This might happen automatically via `updateMatrix()` or manually by the app if `matrixAutoUpdate` is set to `false` (which means the app owns `Object3D.matrix`).
- Nothing ever sets a descendant's `matrixWorldNeedsUpdate` flag in response to an ancestor modification. The way descendants actually stay in sync is the `force` cascade, not the flag. Once any ancestor recomputes, it passes `force = true` down so every descendant recomputes regardless of their own `matrixWorldNeedsUpdate`. The flag on a child plays no role in that propagation.
- That's why `WebGLRenderer.render()` unconditionally calls `scene.updateMatrixWorld()` each frame (when auto update is active), and why the current policy is: _if you change transforms and raycast in the same frame, call `updateMatrixWorld()`/`updateWorldMatrix()` by yourself first_.
- Running `this.updateWorldMatrix( true, false );` unconditionally in `raycast()` like certain `Object3D` methods (`localToWorld()` or `worldToLocal()`) is potentially a huge waste since the world matrix might already be up-to-date. Since the app knows best if this condition is met, it's best to keep `Raycaster` as it is.
- A raycaster-side `matrixWorldNeedsUpdate` guard would give a false sense of safety. It's just an incomplete check. Right now, there is no cheap, reliable per-node "is my world matrix stale" query. This is part of a larger topic discussed in #21387. The world matrix lifecycle and update mechanism need a larger redesign to properly solve this issue.


Merging into #21387.

body:
`OrbitControls.js` imports from the built files, so you were probably including the three.js `src/` twice in your custom build.

To make it work properly, you should add `three` as a dependency to your project (`npm i three`), and have this as the main file of the custom build:

```js
export * from 'three'
export { OrbitControls } from 'three/examples/jsm/controls/OrbitControls.js';
```

Also, this is an help request, I'd advise you to post to the [forum](https://discourse.threejs.org/) instead.

body:
> The skeleton should reset to the initial pose as stored in the glTF asset.

If we overwrite the initial matrices of the joints with bind matrices, we are losing some information ... I'm not sure that's what all users will want here, or that we should consider it a bug to omit.

Also -- I think it's possible for two skinned meshes to rely on the same bones, with different inverse bind matrices.
author: donmccurdy

body:
Thanks for the feedback. What if instead of copying the bind matrices, we add an `.updateMatrixWorld()` so the joint world matrices match their transform in the glTF doc? I think that addresses the points you bring up.

```diff js
// GLTFLoader.js

+ mesh.updateMatrixWorld()

  mesh.bind( new Skeleton( bones, boneInverses ), mesh.matrixWorld );

```

I'm not sure if there are conventions for when joint world matrices should be initialized. If this doesn't belong in the loader, then alternative the update could be added in `Skeleton.pose()` and somewhere in the `.clone()` flow.
author: mattrossman

body:
I believe that should be OK — even without skinning involved, it does seem better if GLTFLoader's results are initialized with correct world matrices when the loader returns.

Ideally, we'd call `updateMatrixWorld()` once on the root of each scene in the glTF file, propagating updates to all nodes in the scene. I'm not sure whether there's a good place to put that call in the loading process, or if the skeleton is constructed before the full scene hierarchy is assembled. 

/cc @takahirox FYI
author: donmccurdy

body:
Looking at the code, the skeletons are indeed created during the node hierarchy build. So there is not a place where world matrices can be computed once for all skeletons.

BTW: If you need correctly computed world matrix, use  `mesh.updateWorldMatrix( true, false );`.
author: Mugen87

body:
If I'm right, the default `bindMode` `"attached"` doesn't use `bindMatrix` (the second argument of `.bind()`), so `bindMatrix` isn't used anywhere and inserting `mesh.updateMatrixWorld()` doesn't have any effect, does it?

https://threejs.org/docs/#api/en/objects/SkinnedMesh.bindMode
https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/0ccddfd057512e611fe5faa0c3eaee99f3036563/src/objects/SkinnedMesh.js#L101-L103

Are you thinking of `detached` `bindMode`? But if we use updated skinned mesh world matrix, doesn't it mismatch the glTF core spec?

> Only the joint transforms are applied to the skinned mesh; the transform of the skinned mesh node MUST be ignored.

https://registry.khronos.org/glTF/specs/2.0/glTF-2.0.html#joint-hierarchy

And found another implementation note in the core spec.

> Implementation Note
The matrix defining how to pose the skin’s geometry for use with the joints (also known as “Bind Shape Matrix”) should be premultiplied to mesh data or to Inverse Bind Matrices.
author: takahirox

body:
So, if I read the spec correctly

> Implementation Note
The matrix defining how to pose the skin’s geometry for use with the joints (also known as “Bind Shape Matrix”) should be premultiplied to mesh data or to Inverse Bind Matrices.

> Only the joint transforms are applied to the skinned mesh; the transform of the skinned mesh node MUST be ignored.

https://registry.khronos.org/glTF/specs/2.0/glTF-2.0.html#joint-hierarchy

I think passing an identity matrix to `SkinnedMesh.bind()` as the second argument seems to match the spec.

And bind matrix doesn't seem to be used in `Skeleton.pose()`.

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/f3149bad3464ad439bef28bda11cb6db55df726f/src/objects/Skeleton.js#L87-L128

I guess the root cause in your case would be that bind shape matrix is not premultiplied to mesh data or to inverse bind matrices in your glTF model?
author: takahirox

body: I think this issue can be closed because `GLTFLoader` follows the core specification. Please reopen with details if it doesn't follow the specification.
author: takahirox

body:
> inserting mesh.updateMatrixWorld() doesn't have any effect, does it?

I didn't understand this part -- `mesh.updateMatrixWorld()` does have an effect from my testing, it fixes the bind pose from being wiped.

I still think there is a bug here, perhaps not with GLTFLoader if it follows the spec but maybe somewhere else. It doesn't make sense to me that [this works](https://jsfiddle.net/mattrossman/0y28oje9/6/) but [this doesn't](https://jsfiddle.net/mattrossman/0y28oje9/7/), the only difference being whether I rendered the scene before posing the skeleton. The bind pose data is there and `.pose()` works most of the time, just not immediately after loading. Furthermore if call `.pose()` directly after loading then the data is lost, and subsequent calls to `.pose()` after rendering don't work.
author: mattrossman

body:
I notice the skeletons produced within GLTFLoader (i.e. those that get bound to the SkinnedMeshes within it) do have a correct bind pose. The difference in my other examples is that I'm calling the Skeleton constructor myself on a list of Bones found in the model.

There's a couple of patterns I now use to access the correct skeleton(s) within a glTF model. On is by searching for a SkinnedMesh:

```js
const skinnedMesh = gltf.scene.getObjectByProperty('isSkinnedMesh', true)
const skeleton = skinnedMesh.skeleton // can safely call .pose() on this
```

A limitation of that approach is it only works for skeletons that are bound to a mesh, not for plain skeletons. A more robust approach is to use the GLTFParser:

```js
const skeleton = await gltf.parser.loadSkin(0) // gltf.json.skins shows available indices
```

Here's an updated fiddle that correct displays the bind pose without explicitly updating world matrices:

https://jsfiddle.net/mattrossman/wa5bz7r8/8/

I didn't see a similar pattern for FBXLoader and didn't check in other loaders, but as long as I'm using GLTFLoader I have some resolution.

author: LeviPesin

body:
> Technically both Three and Babylon are also non-conformant with the another specified behaviour: ...

At least for three.js, this is ultimately up to the application developer. THREE.WebGLRenderer will draw into whatever viewport it is given. My viewer (https://gltf-viewer.donmccurdy.com/) stretches the rendered image to fit the viewport, but another three.js-based viewer could make a different choice here. This feature hasn't been a priority for me, but if I were spending more time on it I think I would implement a letterbox in my viewer instead.

Blender doesn't exactly associate an aspect ratio with each camera, if I remember correctly, which makes this a bit tricky from an import/export perspective.

> Not 100% sure I'm interpreting the spec correctly, but either way something isn't lining up here.

Hm, yeah these results do not look like I would've expected if each square is one unit. Visually, babylon.js does seem to be doing what the spec would suggest for the scene, correct?

body:
@Mugen87 As I understand it, both Blender and `three.js` were handling this incorrectly. Blender was exporting ortho cameras twice as big as they should be (sans aspect ratio). Three appears to then be additionally also displaying ortho cameras frames another two times or so as big as they should be, for a scene that's in total zoomed out by four times. I fixed the behaviour on the Blender export side, but I think that means the Three behaviour is still incorrect.

To test this, I manually edited the `xmag` and `ymag` in the GLTF file I embedded my initial comment here, and then also compared the result to Babylon and to what the spec seems to say. Having just tried that again, this issue still seems to be happening with Three.
author: will-ca

body: Okay. TBH, I still don't understand where to fix this issue. Does `GLTFLoader` need an update? How would this look like?
author: Mugen87

body:
@Mugen87 Honestly, I don't know either.

Steps I'd try:

1. Make sure there actually is an issue. It's possible I'm just misreading the spec. Also, strangely, looking back at some screenshots where I wasn't specifically looking for it, I'm not sure this issue happened consistently. Or maybe what the spec says in its verbal description might not match the projection matrix it actually provides or something. (I did not look too hard into the matrix.) Try the `OrthoScale.gltf` file, and compare its results to what the spec describes. Interestingly, the Khronos sample viewer behaves roughly similarly to Three.JS (and with further self-inconsistencies varying with window scale)— If there is an issue here, then the Khronos viewer needs to be reported too, and if there isn't an issue here, then instead Babylon will need to be fixed and I'll have to update the Blender code instead. Basically, Babylon/Blender are currently behaving differently from Three/Khronos, and I'm *pretty sure* the behaviour in Babylon and Blender is what the spec says should happen, but I would still feel more comfortable if it were checked out by someone else.
2. Assuming that doesn't figure it out, and assuming the documentation for `OrthographicCamera` I cite in the initial comment is correct about how `OrthographicCamera` parameters map to the camera frustum, then the `GLTFLoader` code I cite in the initial comment also *looks* correct, I think. So… I'd check the `OrthographicCamera` object in a live scene then, to see if the parameters passed by `GLTFLoader` to it are actually being set correctly (I.E. or are they being doubled somewhere along the way?). And if that's fine, then I guess I'd manually check whether `OrthographicCamera` is actually behaving as it seems to be described in the documentation I cited, without using `GLTFLoader`.

So, I think we have basically four places to look:

- The GLTF spec says how the glTF ortho camera parameters should be handled.
- The Three.JS docs say how `three.js` `OrthographicCamera` constructor args are supposed to be used.
- The `OrthographicCamera` implementation uses `three.js` `OrthographicCamera` constructor args a certain way.
- And the `GLTFLoader` class is supposed to turn the glTF ortho camera parameters into `three.js` `OrthographicCamera` constructor args in a way that according to the docs should match the spec.

And somewhere along the way, it looks like the numbers are being doubled.
author: will-ca

body:
>And somewhere along the way, it looks like the numbers are being doubled.

```js
"camera" : 0,
"name" : "Camera",
"rotation" : [
    -0.7071068286895752,
    0,
    0,
    0.7071068286895752
],
"scale" : [
    2,
    1.9999998807907104,
    1
],
"translation" : [
    6,
    2,
    -4
]
```
author: WestLangley

body:
@WestLangley  Huh. Well found.

So then I guess the question is what is the correct behaviour? I think this is the relevant section in the spec:

> ### 3.10.2. View Matrix
> The camera is defined such that the local +X axis is to the right, the “lens” looks towards the local -Z axis, and the top of the camera is aligned with the local +Y axis.
> **The view matrix is derived from the global transform of the node containing the camera with the scaling ignored.** If the node’s global transform is identity, the location of the camera is at the origin.

https://registry.khronos.org/glTF/specs/2.0/glTF-2.0.html#view-matrix

~~If I am interpreting that correctly, I suppose it means that the `GLTFLoader` needs to correct for scale on import?~~

…But actually, no, because depending on the parent hierarchy, the camera's global transform/world matrix could still have scaling that is not only `!= 1.0`, but even non-uniform, different over time, and with shearing…


How is that currently handled?
author: will-ca

body:
>the camera's global transform/world matrix could still have scaling that is not only != 1.0, but even non-uniform

Yes, as in your example.

>The view matrix is derived from the global transform of the node containing the camera with the scaling ignored.
>>How is that currently handled?

three.js does not ignore the scaling in a camera transform.

three.js assumes the rotation component of the view matrix is orthonormal.

(There are side-effects if it is not. See, for example, #9002, and the discussion in #7028.)
author: WestLangley

body:
> > The view matrix is derived from the global transform of the node containing the camera with the scaling ignored.
> three.js does not ignore the scaling in a camera transform.

It sounds like that's the root of the issue in this sample then —  we don't ignore scale on a camera. Aside from the difference with the glTF spec, is there any reason we'd want (or not want) that change in the core three.js library? 

Short of that, I guess we could modify GLTFLoader to apply an inverse scale to the camera as a one-time correction, but this wouldn't account for any animation on the camera or its parents. But I don't really like that option much.
author: donmccurdy

body:
>is there any reason we'd want (or not want) that change in the core three.js library?

I would not make it a priority unless there is demand for doing so. Cameras can have children, and ignoring part of the camera's world transform raises questions about how to handle the children.

Cameras in three.js should not have a scale applied.

author: fengxinming

body:
Developing features that work for both WebGL 1 and 2 is not always possible because of certain major API differences. E.g. we can only provide 3D textures or multisampled render targets in WebGL 2 because there is no proper way to provide an emulated solution in WebGL 1.

Also notice that support for WebGL 1 will be removed at some point, see #22689.

I suggest to use the forum to discuss possible strategies for supporting a wide range of devices.
author: Mugen87

body:
> The index of the image used by this texture. When undefined, an extension or other mechanism SHOULD supply an alternate texture source, otherwise behavior is undefined.

How I read this would be that the file is not invalid under the core glTF specification, but when `.source` is omitted, it's very much the expectation that _something_ will be providing that texture's data. Generally that's the extension, and the core specification is just leaving some flexibility for extensions to define implementation details.  With `KHR_texture_basisu` (the only ratified Khronos Group extension currently extending textures) you'll note that the extension itself [specifies](https://github.com/KhronosGroup/glTF/blob/main/extensions/2.0/Khronos/KHR_texture_basisu/README.md#using-without-a-fallback):

> To use KTX v2 image with Basis Universal supercompression without a fallback, define KHR_texture_basisu in both extensionsUsed and extensionsRequired. The texture object will then have its source property omitted as shown below.

In other words, `KHR_texture_basisu` must be marked as required, if assigned to a texture whose base `.source` is undefined. 

***

tl;dr — I think it's OK for us to throw an error here, or at least the specification doesn't say otherwise. If we think it would be better to something else (skip the texture? return a bright purple placeholder texture?) then we could certainly discuss that.
author: donmccurdy

body:
Thanks for the response.

Yeah, it's the best if extension specifications define whether the extensions must be marked as required.

Let me keep this issue opened for a week because someone else might have different opinions.

body:
Penumbra in `three.js` is defined as follows:

>  Percent of the spotlight cone that is attenuated due to penumbra. Takes values between zero and 1. Default is zero.

I don't think it is right to use `Math.min()` instead of `Math.max()` since you would end up with values outside of the `[0,1]` range.

To me, the code in the loader looks correct. It's probably best if you share an FBX assets that demonstrates the issue.
author: Mugen87

body:
> To me, the code in the loader looks correct. It's probably best if you share an FBX assets that demonstrates the issue.

Consider the current code in `FBXLoader.js`:
```js
 	penumbra = Math.max( penumbra, 1 ); 
``` 	
If `penumbra` is greater than 1 then `Math.max()`, which returns the maximum value between a pair of provided input values, will return a value greater than 1.  As you said, the value has to be less than or equal to one (and greater than or equal to zero), so that results in an invalid value if `penumbra` is greater than 1.  If you use use the `min()` function instead, and the value calculated for `penumbra` is greater than 1, The `min()` function will return 1.

From the MDN docs for `Math.max()`:

https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/Math/max

```
The Math.max() function returns the largest of the numbers given as input parameters, or -[Infinity](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/Infinity) if there are no parameters.
```
author: roschler
WestLangley
roschler

body:
It's a while since I looked at this stuff so here's the relevant docs that I could find:

For reference here's [the FBX docs for inner/outer angle](https://help.autodesk.com/view/FBX/2017/ENU/?guid=__cpp_ref_class_fbx_light_html) (can't link directly, search on the page for innerangle):

> InnerAngle: This property handles the light inner cone angle (in degrees). Also know as the HotSpot. Default value is 45.0
> OuterAngle: This property handles the light outer cone angle (in degrees). Also known as the Falloff. Default value is 45.0

The [three.js concepts of angle and penumbra](https://threejs.org/docs/?q=spotli#api/en/lights/SpotLight):

> angle - Maximum angle of light dispersion from its direction whose upper bound is Math.PI/2.
> penumbra - Percent of the spotlight cone that is attenuated due to penumbra. Takes values between zero and 1. Default is zero.

Also, here's the description of [falloff and hotspot from the 3ds max docs](https://knowledge.autodesk.com/support/3ds-max/learn-explore/caas/CloudHelp/cloudhelp/2021/ENU/3DSMax-Lighting-Shading/files/GUID-4A96BEC8-66E5-4DA4-BD78-5E2872A3D2AD-htm.html):

> Hotspot/Beam/_innerangle_: Adjusts the angle of a light's cone. The Hotspot value is measured in degrees. Default=43.0.
> Falloff/Field/_outerangle_: Adjusts the angle of a light's falloff. The Falloff value is measured in degrees. Default=45.0.
> For photometric lights, the Field angle is comparable to the Falloff angle. It is the angle at which the light's intensity has fallen to zero.

From [brownbagfilms.com](https://www.brownbagfilms.com/labs/entry/basic-lighting-in-3ds-max#:~:text=The%20spotlight%20also,disc%20or%20rectangle.) talking about the 3ds max spotlight:

> The spotlight also has its own parameters section. The main things to take into account here are the hotspot/beam and falloff settings. These are fairly straightforward, just think of it as two cones, the inner one being the full brightness (Hotspot) of the spotlight and the outer being the penumbra or falloff, the outer edge of which the light falls off to zero. 

So it seems ok to me that `outerangle = spotlight.angle` and `innerangle = spotlight.penumbra`, with appropriate conversions to radians and ratio. @Mugen87 and @WestLangley, is it correct that the three.js penumbra sets an inner cone of full brightness? 

@roschler given that I think your code is mostly ok, except for the penumbra default. In FBX, it defaults to 45 degrees. That would give a penumbra of 1 in three.js _if_ the angle is also 45 degrees. If it's not, we need to scale it to get the eqivalant ratio for other angles. 

@WestLangley your code is the same minus checks and setting defaults I think. Given that it's FBX though it's a good idea to assume everything that can be badly defined will be and add these ahead of time.

body:
>is it correct that the three.js penumbra sets an inner cone of full brightness?

Yes, but the three.js penumbra attenuation formula probably should be revisited.

body:
> For example to drag groups together while the children stay locked in place.

Um, I don't think you can enforce this by setting `recursive` to `false`. It won't be possible to drag ancestor nodes but they will still be transformed when their top-most node gets dragged.
author: Mugen87

body:
> > For example to drag groups together while the children stay locked in place.
> 
> Um, I don't think you can enforce this by setting `recursive` to `false`. It won't be possible to drag ancestor nodes but they will still be transformed when their top-most node gets dragged.

Sorry if the explanation was not clear. The intention is for the DragControls objects array to control precisely which objects are able to be dragged without allowing their children to be dragged. As you explained it's not a problem for the children to move when the ancestor is dragged since this is intended behavior in the scene graph.

One use case would be having a mesh group as a sphere wrapping around all the children. The ancestor object (a sphere representing a group) would be able to be dragged while the user can't individually drag the children of the sphere since they have a special layout inside the group. So we would have fine-grain control around which children can be dragged if some are locked and some are draggable. If we want the children to be draggable we can add them to the objects array.
author: clintonwoo

body:
Um, I'm not sure about this proposal. Users could find it confusing how the `recursive` flag with a `false` value works (or its general purpose). It seems too use case specific to me.

Right now, I vote to not change `DragControls` unless more users request this feature.
author: Mugen87

body:
You can achieve the same output via layers: https://jsfiddle.net/2ocutr1f/2/

Meaning you receive the internal raycaster of `DragControls` via `DragControls.getRaycaster()`. You then define a layer so only objects with the respective layer enabled are draggable. In the above fiddle its `1`. 

In this way, no modifications to `DragControls` are necessary.

body:
> CurvePath.getPoint(t) returns points based on path length?

A single curve like a line or bezier curve can be defined analytically with a formula. In this case, you can use `t` for point sampling because a single formula describes the entire curve. That is not true for a sequence of curves like when using `CurvePath`. You have to work with length/distance properties to identify the correct curve in the sequence and the sample it based on the computed length value (`u`). Hence, `getPointAt()` is used.

As you have pointed out, introducing a different computation now will affect existing use cases. I also think there is no compelling reason to change the implementation since the usage of `getPointAt()` in `CurvePath.getPoint(t)` is not wrong per se.
author: Mugen87

body: On second thought, when trying to parameterise `CurvePath` by `t`, it comes down to a choice of how much you want to weight each of the child curves, and its just an arbitrary choice from there, and the choice made in this case was to use path length - as opposed to what I assumed before of weighting them equally. So I agree that the current implementation is not wrong.
author: jajetloh

body: Okay, closing then.

body:
Finding a threshold that handles all use cases "correctly" is almost impossible. Even if we double the epsilon, someone could came up with a similar request for an even greater threshold.  

One possible fix is to parametrize the epsilon with `Number.EPSILON` as the default. However, doing this in all methods were an epsilon is used will also clutter the signatures.
author: Mugen87

body:
You _could_ expose it as a parameter, like done here: https://threejs.org/docs/#examples/en/math/OBB.intersectsOBB 

But I guess there's quite a few of these cases throughout threejs 

In this particular case, people can't come with crazy values in though - the dot product is necessarily between -1 and 1, here it should be possible to find a threshold that works for "everyone"

body:
Optional chaining `?.` is an ES2020 feature. Related discussions:

- https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/23763
- https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/22618
- https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/23480

I think we've landed on adding a lint step, that warns us before shipping features that don't have widespread browser support. Optional chaining is widely supported in browsers, and presumably passes the lint for that reason, but it seems that your bundler (webpack 4?) may not support the feature without a plugin like Babel:

https://stackoverflow.com/questions/59972341/how-to-make-webpack-accept-optional-chaining-without-babel

Do you have the option of updating webpack, or using the Babel plugin? I think we'd prefer to use featuresets we can lint against, rather keeping track of individual feature support in older bundlers.

const speed = 100

body:
We do have the _Wrappers and Frameworks_ section in the [Libraries and Plugins](https://threejs.org/docs/#manual/en/introduction/Libraries-and-Plugins) page, and can always add new things there. [A-Frame](https://aframe.io/) is an example of the Web Component approach. Perhaps counterintuitively, I think the overhead (in both performance and maintenance cost) is higher than with the approaches taken by [`@react-three/fiber`](https://github.com/pmndrs/react-three-fiber) or [threlte](https://threlte.xyz/).

As for explaining how to "build your own framework" with Web Components, I think it'd be an interesting case study (blog post, github project, etc.) but would agree it probably isn't something we should maintain within the three.js project.

body:
It sounds like you are experiencing an issue with the display of LDraw models in Three.js on a Windows 10 machine, where certain parts are being displayed as black instead of grey. This issue may be specific to a newer version of Chrome, as it was not present on a Windows 7 machine with an older version of Chrome.

There could be a number of reasons why this issue is occurring. It's possible that there is a bug in the LDrawLoader code that is causing the incorrect display of certain parts. It could also be an issue with the model itself, or with the way that the model is being loaded or rendered in Three.js.

To try to resolve this issue, you could try the following steps:

Make sure that you are using the latest version of Three.js and the LDrawLoader. Sometimes bugs are fixed in newer versions of a library, so updating to the latest version may help.

Check the model file for any errors or issues that could be causing the incorrect display of certain parts. You can use the LDraw Model Viewer to view the model and see if it displays correctly there.

Check the code that is loading and rendering the model in Three.js to make sure that it is correct and that there are no errors or issues that could be causing the incorrect display.

Try using a different browser or a different version of Chrome to see if the issue is specific to the browser or version that you are currently using.

If none of these steps helps, you may want to consider posting a question on a forum or Stack Overflow with more details about the issue and the steps you have already taken to try to resolve it. Providing a minimal, reproducible example of the issue would also be helpful in getting assistance.

author: Mugen87

body:
For a mature project like three.js, high-quality translations (or internationalization / "i18n") feel very important, and I'd be sorry for that area of the project to become stale. Really grateful for the folks doing these translations. If we simply offload translation to "the community" and don't set up a good workflow for keeping things in sync, then I expect the translations may degrade quickly. Our hands-off approach to `@types/three` will not suffice here.

With @Mugen87, I am glad to be involved in this task in any way. This is important to me.

It may be that the current setup is not that great for translators either, and offloading (done well) might actually improve things. For example, a separate repository might allow maintainers of the translations to set up tooling that we haven't provided here, for quickly identifying:

- new strings, not yet translated to language X
- updated strings, changed since translation to language X
- progress, % of documentation translated to language X

I'd love to hear from people doing these translations: What works today? What doesn't? How do you feel having translations occur in a separate repository, possibly with more dedicated tooling to support that? Are there tools for this you've used before, and liked?
author: donmccurdy

body:
>I'd be sorry for that area of the project to become stale.

They are already stale.
author: WestLangley

body:
The maintenance work I do is straightforward right now because when a single documentation page is updated, the changes can often be copy/pasted with an editor (new code example, updated signature etc.). But granted it is not a scalable solution to copy each documentation page and translate the contents by hand. However, using a real translation system with placeholders in the documentation templates and a language database seems out of scope.

I'm not an expert in this field but would it be possible to avoid any manual translation and just ask the developer to use a tool e.g. like Google Translate? Are the results considered to be good enough now?

I've tested this today with some guides from the documentation and the results are about right (at least when you translate into German). I mean the tool translates some words that it shouldn't (like render target) and the structure of a sentence is sometimes a bit weird but at least you understand the actual content of the article.

I wonder if there is a way to improve the English docs so a translation tool can produce better results. Like preventing the translation of certain words or defining their syntax (e.g. that "render targets" should not be translated and is the plural of "render target"). Maybe it would be possible to tweak the results up to a point where it can be considered as "good enough" and a manual translation isn't necessary anymore.
author: Mugen87

body:
> However, using a real translation system with placeholders in the documentation templates and a language database seems out of scope. I wish I knew a practical way to do this, but, yeah. I'm not sure we have the resources. > I wonder if there is a way to improve the English docs so a translation tool can produce better results One option would be to change how we're using `` blocks today. We use them for longform code blocks, but that's really what `
` is for:

```html


import * as THREE from 'three';

...


```

If we change that, we could start using `` for inline tokens instead, like:

```html


  This guide explains how to use GLTFLoader.


```

Then we just need to prevent translation from affecting code tags, but limit syntax highlighting to `
` blocks.
author: donmccurdy

body: It seems doing this: ``` render target ``` prevents Google Translate from translating the words into another language. I've tested this with Chrome and the built-in translating option. The `translate` attribute is not Google Translate specific so other tools should recognize it as well.
author: Mugen87

body:
At least Google Translate does already not translate the code examples in the documentation. I guess this happens because the code blocks already have the `translate="no"` attribute set.
https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/6663480c40f31629c4a0af98d5d2df7072888b45/docs/page.js#L164
author: Mugen87

body: Yeah, it may be more practical to use `<code />` blocks inline, and let the script add `translate="no"`, rather than using a span with attributes each time.
author: donmccurdy

body: Um, I'm just not sure if it's right to use the `<code />` element like that. AFAIK, it's intended to define computer code in HTML files. We just want to mark specific text from not being processed by the translator. Sounds a bit like a misusage to me. To me, the`<span>` tag with an additional attribute feels like the cleaner approach.
author: Mugen87

body:
I only mean using it when we're referring to classes, methods, and variables. It's an inline element, not a block element, in normal usage:

https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Element/code

This helps with styling too, I'm often putting words like THREE.Group in italics to identify them, but we could use consistent styling with semantic markup instead.

But yeah, if we're trying to prevent a non-code word or phrase from being translated, then `<span translate="no" />` is probably better.
author: donmccurdy

body:
> I'm not an expert in this field but would it be possible to avoid any manual translation and just ask the developer to use a tool e.g. like Google Translate? Are the results considered to be good enough now?

Depends on the output language -- e.g. for Russian it's generally good enough for few words or a phrase, but a paragraph can have some problems. But it is understandable.

body:
I would love to help in any way I can, primarily if it makes it easier to maintain doc comments in the TypeScript definitions, since right now I have to manually copy/paste those and reformat them into a JSDoc. I'm not sure that we can fully generate TypeScript definitions directly from the JSDoc quite yet, but I think it could be very useful as a starting point for the TS types.

If the current proposal is acceptable to everyone, I'd be happy to start working on it. I can put together a proof-of-concept branch if that would be helpful. Are we okay with just adding JSDoc comments to start, or do we want to make sure the doc generation part works decently well first?
author: Methuselah96

body: Here's a quick proof-of-concept: https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/29357. In this case the types are simple enough that we could use the generated .d.ts without any modifications, which would be awesome.
author: Methuselah96

body:
> I'm not sure that we can fully generate TypeScript definitions directly from the JSDoc quite yet, ...

Yes, this was the 'blocker' I mentioned in my comment – I'm not sure we could generate acceptable TypeScript definitions from JSDoc ... but I see no reason that generating _documentation_ from JSDoc shouldn't work well. If that's still enough of a win to move forward, then let's do it.

I would also be grateful for a sanity-check from one or more people who are involved in contributing translations – does contributing translated strings into a more machine-readable file (.csv, .yml, or .sqlite?) sound like an improvement over the current structure of writing in .html files? I *think* it would be, because it's much easier to identify English source strings that have yet to be translated, or that have changed since the last translation ... but I'm not translating the content myself, and I want to be sure we're making that easier not harder... 😅
author: donmccurdy

body:
If I understand correctly, the main pain point is contributors and maintainers needing to keep the non-English docs in-sync, so it seems like anything that reduces the need to update the docs in 7 different places would be an improvement.

@donmccurdy Do you have a plan for generating the English-language API docs from JSDoc and/or can I help getting that in place? It seems like it would be nice to have that ready before we start adding the JSDoc so that we're not adding to the duplication of docs. Or if we're fine with the duplication, I can start adding JSDoc, it would still be useful for `three-ts-types`.
author: Methuselah96

body:
@Methuselah96 I think that's a correct statement of the pain point, yes!

If we were confident about getting TS definitions too, I had been thinking of using [ts-morph](https://ts-morph.com/) (I have some experience with this) to parse the definitions into a simple JSON representation of the three.js public API (for example, `api.json`). But there may be other/better tools for extracting a JSON representation of the API from the JSDoc, too.

Then, a script should extract all English-language strings for translation (`docs.en.csv`). Non-English language strings would appear in other files (`docs.cn.csv`). Finally, documentation would be built by a script that processes the JSON, looks up translations from the CSVs, and outputs HTML files for each language in a build step.

I think it would be good to have a proof-of-concept for the full process before we begin committing a lot of JSDoc to the three.js repository, but adding sample JSDoc for one or two files sooner seems OK to me if that's helpful.
author: donmccurdy

body: Per [this comment](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/29357#issuecomment-2345338697), I think we want to steer clear of defining types that are specific to TS (e.g., generics/type parameters or conditional types) in this repo, and focus only on how JSDoc can be used to generate docs. Then in `three-ts-types` I can use the JSDoc types as a starting point, and add TS-specific things like generics/type parameters (used in classes like [EventDispatcher](https://github.com/three-types/three-ts-types/blob/master/types/three/src/core/EventDispatcher.d.ts#L41) and [BufferGeometry](https://github.com/three-types/three-ts-types/blob/master/types/three/src/core/BufferGeometry.d.ts#L117)) and conditional types (used in [TSLCore](https://github.com/three-types/three-ts-types/blob/master/types/three/src/nodes/tsl/TSLCore.d.ts#L57)) as patches on top of that.
author: Methuselah96

body:
> It would be helpful if someone would be willing to take responsibility for this task.

You called 😇 @WestLangley 

#30245 has been labelled as a duplicate of this issue so I shall summarize below.

My contributing idea is simple:

* Introduce a [mono-repo/workspaces](https://docs.npmjs.com/cli/v7/using-npm/workspaces) setup for docs in the repository and
* Adopt [Astro-starlight](https://starlight.astro.build/getting-started/) as three.js's documentation website framework

The [migration](https://docs.astro.build/en/guides/migrate-to-astro/) would not be about changing the look or styling of the docs and `.astro` files are basically a superset of html.

[Astro](https://docs.astro.build/en/getting-started/) itself is successfully running i18n documentation with 14 different languages in-sync with their development. Markdown as a source file format is more approachable for contributors (and maintainers) and starlight has a ton of [tools](https://starlight.astro.build/guides/authoring-content/) for content authors & i18n support.
author: DefinitelyMaybe

body:
At this point, I vote to stop i18n support in the project. `three.js` should focus on providing a auto-generated English documentation via JSDoc. Similar to the maintenance of TS declaration files, the maintenance of translated documentation pages should be managed in a separate repository.

With the migration to JSDoc, we lay the groundwork for new translation workflows. How they will end up looking is something that should be managed elsewhere.

body:
Thanks for the comments! 
Sounds like the global may be the most useful solution here. Typically most textures do indeed have the same aniso value in an app.

Agree that it would be great if there's a regular way to get all textures that were loaded independent of extension; would you consider it a bug that `associations` / `getDependency` contain the wrong texture for `texture_transform` cases?

Iterating over material keys sounds doable but rather slow, plus there may be another fancy case: we have an extension that sets up custom shaders during loading, so theoretically one needs to traverse uniforms as well to really find all textures that way.
author: hybridherbst

body:
> would you consider it a bug that associations / getDependency contain the wrong texture for texture_transform cases?

For `getDependency` no (it's intended to return source textures in the file, not all variants cloned while parsing), but for `associations` yes.

I'm happy with the default idea suggested by @takahirox and @WestLangley  as well. (https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/25015)

body:
Textures with no image data are a bit special. The renderer does no complain if you assign `new Texture()` to a map property, however, the shader will sample from an empty definition resulting in 0 values (or black color). In most cases this visually breaks the material. Example: https://jsfiddle.net/sct9anv8/

I would also like to know how (and why) an app produces assets with empty texture data.
author: Mugen87

body:
At least the FBXLoader produces such files (it creates Textures and loads them async after returning) - I think if any loader can bring that kind of data in, it's safe to assume that someone will try exporting it again. This is exactly how I stumbled upon this issue. Visually everything looked correct.

When loading such a file with FBXLoader, an error is already logged on image loading, and since users could assign empty textures anyway I thought it's better to guard against the result.

Other options here would be:
- Fixing FBXImporter (only solves it for that case)
- Forcing people to manually sanitize after every loader (not great)
author: hybridherbst

body:
> The renderer does no complain if you assign new Texture() to a map property, however, the shader will sample from an empty definition resulting in 0 values (or black color). In most cases this visually breaks the material. Example: https://jsfiddle.net/sct9anv8/

But doesn't this change if `Texture.DEFAULT_IMAGE` is used in app?
author: LeviPesin

body:
> But doesn't this change if Texture.DEFAULT_IMAGE is used in app?

Of course you can add an image to `Texture.DEFAULT_IMAGE` but the question is if assets look good with it. And if you want these default data in your exports.

> Fixing FBXImporter (only solves it for that case)

If you use `LoadingManager` in combination with `FBXLoader`, you can wait until all resources have loaded and don't run into this issue. There is some sort of silent agreement in the past to not update existing loaders to behave like `GLTFLoader` and `ObjectLoader`. These two loaders are the only ones which wait until all resources have been loaded. 

I suggest you use `LoadingManager` on app level and you won't have any issues with empty texture data (assuming the data itself are not corrupt or something similar). This is how the library is supposed to be used.
author: Mugen87

body: Related issue: #12105
author: Mugen87

body:
> This is how the library is supposed to be used.
You mean it's by design that these loaders work differently and require different patterns to be used? 

I thought one goal of the library was to have easy and consistent patterns 🧐

I'll look into the manager approach, thanks! 

Still think it's weird that it's so easy to break exporters and other code. Independent of what other loaders do I think this check makes sense (with any kind of code validation, this would need to be checked too - the Texture type simply doesn't have image as field).
author: hybridherbst

body: As @donmccurdy suggested, some sort of check does not hurt. I suggest to log a warning and then ignore the respective texture slot.
author: Mugen87

body:
> You mean it's by design that these loaders work differently and require different patterns to be used? I thought one goal of the library was to have easy and consistent patterns 🧐

The loaders evolved over twelve years now and some users rely on the existing behavior of `FBXLoader`, `ColladaLoader` or `OBJ/MTLLoader`. Meaning the ability to display assets as fast as possible, even when no texture data are available yet. `LoadingManager` allows us to support the old way but provides a solution for devs who want to wait until all loaders are done with their work.
author: Mugen87

body:
I'll take a look if I can add that to #25021!

Just to clarify, the issue is not the loading order but that the resulting textures don't have image data. Just using the loading manager doesn't solve that, right? But it gives an opportunity to sanitize data after loading I guess.
author: hybridherbst

body:
> Just using the loading manager doesn't solve that, right?

If you access the asset in `LoadingManager.onLoad()`, all texture data should be fully loaded (so with defined `image` data). Of course you have the opportunity to sanitize at that point, too, if required.
author: Mugen87

body:
Sorry, should have clarified: the issue with the textures is similar to what's described in https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/25014#issuecomment-1328013700. In some cases textures fail to load, are corrupt, etc., in which case the textures returned from FBXLoader have missing image data (they start with missing image data and it's never filled with anything). These in turn cause exceptions in other exporters.

Here's an example file: [Low Poly-1.0.0.zip](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/files/10096048/Low.Poly-1.0.0.zip)

I can open a separate issue about "FBXLoader: loaded textures can be empty" if you want, but I don't have an immediate idea on how to fix that in there.
author: hybridherbst

body:
> These two loaders are the only ones which wait until all resources have been loaded.

I think if maintainer(s) of any particular loader wanted to update it to await all resources before returning, that would be great. But if the handful of core maintainers were also responsible for making updates like these to all of three.js' loaders, we'd be able to maintain only a small fraction of the loaders three.js currently has.
author: donmccurdy

body: Closing. As decided in #25022, exporter will not tolerate uninitialized textures. It's open to decide whether exporters throw exceptions or save a default texture and report a warning, see #25093.

body:
**parseTrackName()** uses regex to separate many different pieces of information. The trouble arises in that `nodeName` can include `.` and there's no easy way to tell where the nodeName ends and where the properties begin (w/o bruteforce check). 

So given trackName `bang.foo.bar.biz` all of the following scenarios are possible:

1. nodeName is `bang` and properties are`foo, bar, biz`
2. nodeName is `bang.foo` and properties are `bar, biz`
3. nodeName is `bang.foo.bar` and property is `biz`

---

I want to propose that a new method named `parseTrackObj()`

This method accepts a json-object as a way to explicitly define info currently being parsed via regex (*nodeName, objectName, objectIndex, propertyChain, propertyKey*).

The goal is to eliminate the need to rely on regex hackery, allow for deeply nested target properties, and generally make things easier to maintain/extend.
author: Bug-Reaper

body:
I'm confused why the additional `setMask()` calls are required. They were originally not added in `MaskPass` since it should be sufficient to just unlock color and depth buffer. `WebGLState.setMaterial()` is called per render item and executes `setMask()` for both color and depth buffer:

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/7fa8637df3edcf21a516e1ebbb9b327136457baa/src/renderers/webgl/WebGLState.js#L757-L758

Meaning as long as the buffers are unlocked and `depthWrite` and `colorWrite` are set to `true`, the WebGL commands `gl.colorMask()` and `gl.depthMask()` will be called with `true` as well.
author: Mugen87

body: Wait, I think I understand now. Color and depth buffer can only be cleared if they are writable. `setMask()` is potentially called to late which breaks the clear.

</script>

body:
@mrdoob you can see the project's score, as well as the results for individual checks, via the API: https://api.securityscorecards.dev/projects/github.com/mrdoob/three.js

In summary, it has detected that:

- a handful of PRs were merged without review (I believe that's #25055, for example)
- both  'dev' and 'master' lack branch protection
- the project lacks a security policy regarding how vulnerabilities can be responsibly disclosed
- the CI workflow could hash-pin its GitHub Action dependencies (`actions/checkout@1254ab3...` instead of `@v3`, for example).
- the project isn't fuzzed

The Action currently doesn't have official support for running on PRs (that feature is due for release next quarter), it instead runs on every push to master (or dev) and populates the Security Dashboard with alerts such as the one shown above if it detects any missteps.
author: pnacht

body: Not sure about how I feel about imposing these "best practices" into the project.
author: mrdoob

body: Fair enough. Could you let me know what your concerns are? That'd be valuable feedback for the Scorecard team.
author: pnacht

body:
It's very hard to imagine what secuity vulnerabilities could be in three.js as library (not `threejs.org` website). three.js does not deal with any secure information - neither reads, nor produces.
(The only potential vulnerabilities that I can imagine are performance vulnerabilities - like, some possibilities of catastrophic backtracking or an infinite loop somewhere, and fuzzing can help with detecting these)
author: LeviPesin

body:
Hey @LeviPesin, thank you.

Just to clarify, the issues that Scorecard focuses on aren't about detecting vulnerabilities in the three.js library itself (it isn't a traditional static/dynamic analysis tool), but the protocols that the project has to prevent the codebase from being hijacked or maliciously modified.

As an example, should a maintainer's account be compromised, an attacker could push malicious code directly to the dev branch hidden in a "fix typo" commit (the classic use-case being for crypto-mining). Activating branch protection would mitigate this risk by ensuring everything goes through PRs. PRs can then be configured such that the PR author (even a maintainer) can't merge their own PRs.

These are the sorts of suggestions that Scorecard aims to make.
author: pnacht

body:
Thanks @pnacht, a few thoughts in context here. There's a frustrating history in web tooling of presumed security vulnerabilities being flagged by systems like `npm audit`, and surfaced _directly to end-users_ in ways that (1) lack any understanding of project's context, and (2) offer no ways to provide that context. [npm audit: Broken by Design](https://overreacted.io/npm-audit-broken-by-design/) is a good critique. As a maintainer I waste a lot of time trying to explain false positives<sup>1</sup> to users who reasonably expect security audits to come back empty, or 'fixing' issues that have no plausible value to the project.

I understand Scorecard has different goals, and may be doing much better than `npm audit` in signal-to-noise ratio. Thank you for that, if so! I agree with most recommendations on the Scorecard, with a few exceptions. But to some extent, I feel inundated by noise already and cautious of introducing more as a maintainer. If there's room for incrementally "opting in" to checks slowly, that may be more appealing for us.

***

<sup>1</sup> In contrast, I don't believe I've ever discovered an actual vulnerability through `npm audit`. 😕
author: donmccurdy

body:
Hey @donmccurdy, I get you. And yeah, Scorecard tries to keep the best possible signal/noise ratio (though we'll be the first to admit it isn't perfect!).

However, also note that the Scorecard info is already public (see the API link I shared earlier). The Action simply makes that info easier for maintainers to monitor in the Security Dashboard (which users can't see).

That being said, I absolutely understand if you feel there's already too much noise! If so, just close the issue and I'm grateful for all the feedback!

body:
I could narrow down the issue a bit. It seems changing the following line:
https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/17386766a77b7916bbaa45aba9e9922d5b81d728/src/renderers/shaders/ShaderChunk/cube_uv_reflection_fragment.glsl.js#L88
to 
```
highp vec2 uv = getUV( direction, face ) * ( faceSize - 2.0 ) + 1.0; 
```
fixes the issue, too. Demo: https://jsfiddle.net/6wxabnkq/4/

So it seems the (subsequent) uv computation breaks in some way with `mediump`.

body:
I would generally love an API design that gives more control of the rendering/culling process by putting the implementations for gathering the render lists and making draw calls into methods of the specific classes, so the component can decide how to handle it, if static or dynamic.

I made a class for instanced skinnning with individual animation states (also with geometry LOD, culling and automatic bone reduction/skin-remapping LOD)
https://discourse.threejs.org/t/animated-instanced-skinned-meshes-gltf/41958/7
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/28584767/205687871-81b18ae1-94f3-47e5-8838-4456c678a0df.png)
(It's basically done i just still don't have a good open test asset that fully utilizes it for a good demo with multiple geometry LODs, would like to make a meta-human detail kind of demo afap)

Other than InstancedMesh regular SkinnedMesh instances are linked to this component  and the SkinnedMesh is not removed from the scene hierarchy and can be nested anywhere. This way the component handles the batching witch culling and LOD automatically, while instancing for the meshes can be turned on/off seamlessly. The most expensive part for SkinnedMesh is the animation and matrices computations which, without a LOD system, makes instancing not really a benefit as performance likely suffers earlier from that than draw calls.

There often is a need that you need to render a instance individually again, like highlighting in a emissive color without making that an additional attribute. So a flexible variant to switch between makes it much more intuitive.


> Make Instanced API for each object type. eg. InstancedSkinnedMesh, InstancedLine, InstancedPoints. But I don't think it's a good idea because of complexity.

I think separating between "Instanced" and "SkinnedInstanced" would be enough, the only complexity difference is there i suppose.

body:

Anything that makes this use case more streamlined has my vote.

https://github.com/bestiejs/benchmark.js/issues/47

author: diarmidmackenzie

body:
EDITED to reflect my updated views on whether this solves the contamination issue (it doesn't).

I have done some experimentation with jsbench.me.  It had some advantages, but I'm not convinced it's adequate to *replace" the current benchmarks.  Perhaps it could be a useful complement.

Here's an example test, derived from the updateMatrixWorld() example I've been working on:
https://jsbench.me/velbwaakvn/1

What's good about this:
- It does longer/slower runs, which I suspect give more accurate results (although I suspect existing THREE.js Benchmarks could be tuned to achieve this).
- Tests are public, so anyone can access them & tinker with them.  Login needed only to save changes to a test.
- No requirement to clone the THREE.js repo locally to run benchmark tests.

What's not good:
- Although it re-runs the test setup steps for each test on a page, this does not solve the contamination/ordering issue.  That could presumably be solved by having a separate page for every single test - but we could equally apply that solution to the existing THREE.js Benchmarks.
- Harder to test against local un-published code.  The solution I have been using for this is ngrok to publish my local build directory on a publically accessible URL, so that I can pull it into the test.  This works, but it's fiddlier to set up than the current benchmarks.
- Performance Profiling is harder.  I can't pause between test setup & test execution.  This means that I can't easily get performance profiling data of just the test from Chrome Dev Tools (I expect this is possible with careful setting of breakpoints, but it would take some effort to figure out correct positioning of breakpoints).
- Version control of test definitions.  Tests can be saved & forked, but we won't have a rich version history like we do with code stored in GitHub.
- Tests take longer to run than current benchmarks.  This is in part the downside of the tests being more accurate, also a consequence of the fact that setup is re-run for each individual test.
- Output data is displayed less compactly.  There's no easy way to copy/paste the data from a run into e.g. a GitHub issue (e.g. via screenshotting or similar).

Other notable differences:
- jsbench.me is based around the assumption that the various tests in a suite are intended for comparison with each other - it reports performance deltas between tests.  That doesn't fit with current benchmark tests, which have fast small-population tests alongside slow large-population tests, and where the interesting comparisons are between one run (against version A of code) and another run (against version B of the code)

Overall, I think jsbench.me is great for use cases where someone wants to compare the performance of a couple of different ways of doing things within THREE.js.  Having some samples linked from the wiki for this purpose could be a valuable addition.

I think it's not well-suited to a master set of THREE.js benchmarks, which can be used to assess performance of the library itself, and the likely impact of changes to THREE.js on delivered performance.

For this purpose, I think it would be better to build on & refine the existing Benchmark tests.

author: dangarfield

body:
> However, in WebGL 2 this is apparently not possible anymore, since the texture size is immutable.

Correct, because we use `texStorage2D()` now, see #21874 and related PRs.

I'm not sure about this feature request. In `three.js` we do in general not support "half-done" textures. A initial texture object has an internal version of `0` because `needsUpdate` has never been set to `true`. As soon as you do that, you tell the engine the texture has been fully loaded and is ready for upload and usage. The engine performs now the combination of the memory allocation via `texStorage2D()` and the initial data upload via `texSubImage2D()`. Subsequent updates only trigger the `texSubImage2D()` because the memory for the texture has already been allocated.

The suggested `suppressInitialTextureUpload` property would bypass the initial call of `texSubImage2D()`. However, I'm not sure the specific state of the texture at that point is something we want to support in `three.js`. IMO, it would mean something like "the memory for the texture is allocated but the data are still incomplete so it is no ready for rendering". Why would you use/render such a type of texture? I would expected to have default data in any event which are possibly later replaced via `texSubImage2D()`.
author: Mugen87

body:
Thank you for your reply!

> In three.js we do in general not support "half-done" textures.

> However, I'm not sure the specific state of the texture at that point is something we want to support in three.js. IMO, it would mean something like "the memory for the texture is allocated but the data are still incomplete so it is no ready for rendering".

> I would expected to have default data in any event which are possibly later replaced via texSubImage2D().

I understand this stance, but would like to clarify that a texture is not "half-done" but instead simply null-initialized after using `texStorage2D` (at least, if I understand [the spec](https://registry.khronos.org/webgl/specs/latest/2.0/#:~:text=The%20image%20contents%20are%20set%20as%20if%20a%20buffer%20of%20sufficient%20size%20initialized%20to%200%20would%20be%20passed%20to%20each%20texImage2D) correctly). For me, this is analoguous to an allocation of a data array à la `new Uint32Array(10)` which is also usable and "done" after allocating without passing some initial data explicitly.

> Why would you use/render such a type of texture?

In my case a completely zeroed texture is perfectly valid and usable (the application I'm developing renders data that is streamed in a chunked manner; if no data is available the data texture is empty and the shader can deal with that). As soon as the first data chunks arrive from the server, the texture is populated incrementally and the first chunks can be rendered as soon as possible. Note that due to the chunked loading + texSubImage2D, there is never a need to have a huge buffer that mirrors the texture data 1:1.

----------------

To frame the issue a bit differently: There is currently no elegant way to zero-initialize textures efficiently in ThreeJS. Even though, `texSubImage2D` does this automatically, one has to allocate a big zeroed buffer to upload that.

> For example, texture.image.suppressInitialTextureUpload = true could be set on the texture to skip the upload call.

Alternatively, one could also think about a placeholder object. Something like `THREE.EmptyTextureImage(width, height)` which could be passed as the `image` property for a texture.

I'm not 100% satisfied with my interface suggestions yet, but iterating on a solution probably makes only sense if you agree that the feature should be added at all :)
author: philippotto

body:
Happy new year! I just wanted to share how we solved this problem internally and how ThreeJS could incorporate this in a simple manner.

At the moment, we went with monkey-patching (:see_no_evil:) `texSubImage2D`, so that it does a no-op if an array buffer of length 0 is passed. Then, we can initialize the image object with something like `{ width, height, data: new Uint32Array(0) }` and the initial texture upload is skipped essentially (thus, avoiding the webgl errors).

ThreeJS could do the `image.data.length === 0` check in `uploadTexture` and skip the `texSubImage2D` call in that case, since the call would fail anyway (unless the texture size is actually 0, but a) that doesn't really make sense and b) this could be checked for, too). The semantics of an empty initial array would then be, that the texture is updated/populated later in another manner.

If you are interested, I could open a PR for this to make my suggestion clearer and to make the discussion easier.
author: philippotto

body:
There is `options.compression` available now, to configure zip compression.

I'm not sure if we'd want to support a wider set of compression codecs, that can be complicated both in terms of maintenance and packaging, as the compression libraries are often large third-party bundles. If you have the option of using something like imagemagick or OIIO, those are solid.

I'm very curious about the ZStandard results discussed with OpenEXR here though — https://github.com/AcademySoftwareFoundation/openexr/issues/1002

body:
Apologies for taking this long to reply, I honestly forgot about this issue.

I don't have plans to include support for different compression levels. Although possible, it is not discussed or addressed in the official OpenEXR specification, and it could lead to problems when loading files in different software.

I do plan to include some of the missing compression algorithms, my goal is to give support to the same compression options available in the loader, namely:

- [x] ZIP (done)
- [x] ZIPS (done)
- [ ] RLE
- [ ] PIZ
- [ ] PXR24
- [ ] DWAA/DWAB

Sorry, but we can't do that. The issue you have describe can happen with any kind of 3D object. If we start to update matrices inside `add()`, we introduce potential performance regressions (especially for apps which frequently add and remove nodes to the scene graph).

If you manually setup skinned meshes, you have to know this details and manage your code accordingly.

body:
I can reproduce. The error appears with [r122](https://raw.githack.com/mrdoob/three.js/r122/editor/index.html) for the first time. At that time, the editor starts to support animation export with `GLTFExporter` (#20454). So the bug probably already existed in the exporter but did not become apparent.

Interestingly, the error only occurs with two or more morphed assets. Non-animated assets work as expected as well as a the combination of a one morphed and one skinned mesh.
author: Mugen87

body:
I expect that this issue (#25179) and #25198 have the same cause. three.js animations reference their targets by a unique name. Importing two assets into the Editor, then exporting, may cause the animation from one model to be in conflict with another. This is fine in normal usage (scope an AnimationMixer to each model) but would require renaming objects for export in the Editor.

GLTFExporter probably cannot fix this; given an AnimationClip list with non-unique names, it's too late to figure out which clip goes where. Maybe this is something we fix in the Editor by prefixing clip names, or by warning users to rename things, or ... ?
author: donmccurdy

body:
I have a few questions:

The goal is to enforce 2018 syntax across all source and examples ?

The plugin `eslint-config-mdcs` enforces 2020 syntax for the last 3 years, does that align ?  Is it still maintained, will you keep the dependency ?  The rules can be copied over if you want to keep the functionality but drop the dependency.

A few observations:

I didn't find an `ecmaVersion` defined in the `eslintConfig` section of the `package.json`.  Ommitting the other settings for clarity, it should look like:

```json
  "eslintConfig": {
    "parserOptions": {
      "ecmaVersion": 2018
    },
  },
```

If you set eslint to 2018, it correctly errors on line 89 of `Three.js`, because it is 2020 syntax as shown below.

```js
export * as AnimationUtils from './animation/AnimationUtils.js';
```

Some options:

- Set `ecmaVersion` to 2020 and be done with it.  It's 2023, existing tools are enforcing 2020, code has 2020 features, 'optional chaining' could simplify lots of the code base (see below).  If the project has been shipping this without complaints, you might be in safe territory.  

- ecmaVersion version to 2018 and update `Three.js` explicitly export `AnimationUtils` classes.

What are your thoughts ?  We can make lots of progress on this with a very little code.


Optional Chaining: (Taken from Wikipedia)

Optional chaining makes it possible to access the nested properties of an object without having an AND check at each level. An example is `const zipcode = person?.address?.zipcode`. If any of the properties are not present, `zipcode` will be `undefined`.
author: epreston

body:
I think the idea is that the build process will compile the _imports and exports_ appropriately — there's only one file in the bundle at the end of the day, that's Rollup's job. The rest of the codebase, excluding imports and exports, should use ES2018. Is that an option we can configure ESLint for?

My general feeling is that we don't really need features like nullish coalescing right now, the DX improvement isn't meaningful at this time, and some tools have broken when these features have gone into the build accidentally. We just want to prevent that from happening again — using ES2020 is a non-goal for now.
author: donmccurdy

body:
@donmccurdy yes, just add the setting above to your package.json with a value of 2018.  The Linter's parser fails at language features above that.  It overrides any plugins that are specifying something else.  In turn people get warnings in their tools and the PR's are checked against 2018 before review.

As far as the build tools go, the project is bundling for a brower support target not a language support target.  The syntax of the bundle is determined by this dynamic setting which changes every quarter:

```
  "browserslist": [
    "> 1%, not dead, not ie 11, not op_mini all"
  ],
```

I'm happy to  take the time to translate that back to language level (plus or minus) language features.
author: epreston

body: You have pointed out options, you can go 2020 and exclude the features you want to avoid with a few quick rules.  The parser option used above is kind of a hard upper bound that we can retreat from.
author: epreston

body:
Writing this out for clarity so I can build a PR today that meets this 150 release goal:

- Developers write 2018 code without any of the bug prone language features that you want to avoid.
- Keep the `eslint-config-mdcs` plugin because it only contains style features and we can override the permissive language level specified in it's `env` config using line in the projects `package.json` as above.
- Build continues to bundle for browser target (which may contain any set of language features to meet it's goals)
- If there's anything meaningful in more recent language levels (handy import syntaxes like in the example above) , implement as 2020 stripped backwards, if not: implement as 2018 and remediate any linter issues.

How's that sound ?
author: epreston

body: I'm hoping for an ESLint configuration that warns us about use of features newer than ES2018, _except_ import/export statements which may use more recent syntax.

body:
> I'm hoping for an ESLint configuration that warns us about use of features newer than ES2018, except import/export statements which may use more recent syntax.

After some tests by @epreston, the best way would be to achieve this result would be to use ES2020 + [eslint-plugin-escompat](https://github.com/keithamus/eslint-plugin-escompat) with the rules `no-nullish-coalescing` and `no-optional-chaining`.

I believe the other features from ES2019/ES2020 are not that relevant to this repo.

author: epreston

body:
It's definitely our intention that the order of children should be deterministic. That said, we can't always guarantee order will be unchanged from one release to the next, so if you're able to use other means to identify things (name, type, .userData, ...) I would strongly recommend that approach.

Looking at the code it's not obvious to me why the order wouldn't be deterministic now, and I can't reproduce the issue in my own tests, so if you're able to share an example (or just a .glb) that would certainly help!

/cc @takahirox
author: donmccurdy

body:
> Looking at the code it's not obvious to me why the order wouldn't be deterministic now, and I can't reproduce the issue in my own tests

I've also checked the issue and could not produce a non-deterministic behavior with `GLTFLoader`. Every time I load glTF assets their node hierarchy is the same.

I guess the OP referred to the fact that the node hierarchy has changed _between_ releases. That is true because of the latest changes applied to `GLTFLoader`. However, these were necessary/reasonable. That said, I don't expect to see changes in the future influencing the node order again. #25058 aligned the order in `three.js` and glTF so I think that is the behavior we want to retain from now on.

Maybe we should add a note to the migration guide for `r148`. Something like:

> `GLTFLoader` now ensures the node order in loaded scenes matches the node order in the glTF asset.

npx madge nodes --circular --warning

author: epreston

body:
A possible solution to the circular dependency issue is described in https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/25240#issuecomment-1417067122 -- the following comments are discussing another architecture change that is not so related to this issue.

> Client code is calling a function in an what looks the interpreter sees as an array index, if I understand it correctly. 

No -- the second suggested change (not that one that directly solves circular dependency issue but that one that allows extending the nodes lib) is just addition of something like `nodeLib[ 'ArrayElementNode' ] = ArrayElementNode` (or `nodeLib.ArrayElementNode = ArrayElementNode`, which is slightly less readable).

If you are talking about using e.g. `element/float`/other functions -- it wouldn't break type checking if the types include them and also shouldn't break autocompletion.

It may break if functions are used as a node methods, though -- and also break when using other benefits of proxying -- like accessing custom properties (`.x` or `.y`) or array indexing... But this should be discussed in a separate issue, I think -- either https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pull/25074 or a new issue.
