body:
Hi, I haven't been here in a minute so I'm probably out of the loop... But, why do you think that this should be part of three.js? 

Composition should work with regular js, ie:

```
const myDAG = new MyWhatever(new MyWhatever('foo'))
```

Also you should be able to just unroll the DAG and get regular js/glsl that three **already** understands. In other words, having two node (nocode?) systems may bloat _this_ project, when it could possibly be an (un)related project, in it's own repo. 

If it's of any interest, i played with these topics a bit. I found it fairly trivial to implement an editor (although mine got really complicated :( ) and export three.js materials. It's very hard, maybe even impossible to generate a shader for three though, because it needs to be generated for a specific version range. 
https://dusanbosnjak.com/test/nodes2/

It is also ( i think ) possible to remove all materials from three.js and leave just `ShaderMaterial` or at least it was possible up to 120 or 130 or so. 
https://github.com/pailhead/three-refactor-chunk-material

So, what i'm trying to say, rather than (IMHO) bloating this project, it's actually possible to make it leaner and more specific to a set of problems. In the case of materials it's just validating GLSL. Three did a decent job formatting that GLSL with just string manipulation and regexs, it did not require the whole nodes system.

And then, i think it's possible to make the whole material system much leaner. There is no need for three to know about "Basic" or "Lambert" material, it could know just about "Material". 

My reasoning is that working with nodes requires a nocode editor, otherwise it feels like bloat to me. Just my 2 cents :)
author: pailhead

body:
The main benefit of the nodes system is flexibility.
You cannot really change an internal function in a GLSL chunk without rewriting it and including as another one. And it will be prone to break on every release.
With nodes system it is just a few lines of JS code (or raw GLSL if you want).
Nodes system doesn't produce code as clean as hand-written indeed... But that can be mostly fixed by inserting `label( ..., name )` at places to make variables variables in GLSL.

> My reasoning is that working with nodes requires a nocode editor, otherwise it feels like bloat to me.

There are such -- apart from Three.js' [base editor](https://threejs.org/editor) there is the [node editor](https://threejs.org/examples/webgpu_nodes_playground.html).

(By the way, I think this isn't very related to this issue)
author: LeviPesin

If you are proposing more systems (such as nodes) to be part of some future renderer or even `/core` I believe that I'm on the right topic. If i'm totally misunderstanding this, then apologies.

body:
I think thats fair, but I think the point here is that if you put a jet engine inside of three it could fly, but why, does it need to?


I think this allows you do do gpgpu with just JavaScript, it’s very old though:
https://github.com/zadvorsky/three.bas

Back in the day three was mostly concerned about rendering. Granted, this was never quite clear, but I think a lot of folks agreed that there is a “renderer”. 

So taking this BAS, zadvorsky was able to create this tool off to the side and use it to whatever it’s supposed to do. He also shared it so others can do that too. But it doesn’t impact the threejs project at all. 

My general take is that a lot of people use threejs for different things, and there are some (probably a minority) that would like threejs to be lean. For example I hated importing all the PBR shaders if all I’m using is my own simple shader material, but cest la vie :)

I think that in theory you could build any kind of a tool you want in parallel with threejs, and that it could actually yield cleaner code do to separation of concerns. 

Again I apologize if there is something I don’t understand. It seems that threejs is much more about nodes today than it was in the past so im not sure if my arguments here are valid.
author: pailhead

body:
> I think this allows you do do gpgpu with just JavaScript, it’s very old though:

I think the concept is a little different from `three.bas`. I think the Node System mission allows you to code TSL and Nodes in any backend or for any need like GPGPU computations. The same code programmed using TSL (Nodes) should work on WebGPU/WebGL and derivatives renderers. NodeMaterial uses the Node System in its base to build the shaders.

> It's very hard, maybe even impossible to generate a shader for three though, because it needs to be generated for a specific version range.

In fact, this is one of the Node System solutions in relation to ShaderChunk.

> I think that in theory you could build any kind of a tool you want in parallel with threejs, and that it could actually yield cleaner code do to separation of concerns.

It is certainly a possibility, I think that over time we add some things and eliminate others and this is part of the innovation and maintenance process. This makes a lot of sense to me as a No-Code Node Graph, @LeviPesin saw another possibility.
author: sunag

body:
> In fact, this is one of the Node System solutions in relation to ShaderChunk.

Just to clarify this - it wouldn’t address this in old versions of three that still rely on chunks? At some point if chunks go away in favor of nodes you are saying they would be compatible with different renderers?

If so, I’d argue that the contract is still just a valid, or somewhat valid GLSL string. My reasoning is that there could be several different node standards or projects that could output this string. I never quite understood why three needs to know about the source of this string.

I think the shader incompatibility is just in the nature of this R versioning system. There is nothing indicating that a certain chunk changed in a certain version, nor why did it change. 

Honestly I think that this issue would be present whatever the system is, because if the renderer changes how it interprets something wouldn’t a node ultimately run into the same issue as a chunk? At the end of the day, both would produce a string, and this string would cause an error?

body: @pailhead Comparing strings is a rationale for ShaderChunk not Node System, WebGPURenderer does not have ShaderChunk, there are no strings to compare there, there are no hacks, so it does not suffer from these problems.

Maybe worth clarifying that i was thinking of stuff like this:

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/f83bc569da527d2821a428ffaead8b29b5f847a5/src/renderers/webgl/WebGLProgram.js#L182
https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/f83bc569da527d2821a428ffaead8b29b5f847a5/src/renderers/webgl/WebGLProgram.js#L199
https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/f83bc569da527d2821a428ffaead8b29b5f847a5/src/renderers/webgl/WebGLProgram.js#L258

I think tone mappings fall into this pattern. Basically what i see here are _things_ that are not necessarily related to a material. Eg. how many lights are in the scene is not something a material knows, neither if it's a `ShaderMaterial` or a `NodeMaterial`. Again, i may be looking at old architecture, so not sure how relevant this is. But logically i'm thinking, lights are something i add to a scene, and materials are something i add to meshes, something needs to reconcile these two at some point.

I don't actually know what "chunks" refer to, i think of that collection of strings that can be assembled into shaders, but i see a lot of variables named `chunks` in this code. I was not referring to those big "chunks" just these things that the renderer appends or switches out. My assumption was that this code would remain in some form, i may be totally wrong about this.
author: pailhead

body:
> I don't actually know what "chunks" refer to, i think of that collection of strings that can be assembled into shaders, but i see a lot of variables named chunks in this code. I was not referring to those big "chunks" just these things that the renderer appends or switches out.

This approch is discontinued in WebGPURenderer, Node System does not use any type .replace() or indexOf() in its finalization, that's why we have Node API for Lights for example. Normally these string manipulations are hacks, that's because we forced the creation of a resource that wasn't contemplated at the API level, that's why we have so many side effects.
author: sunag

body:
> I don't actually know what "chunks" refer to, i think of that collection of strings that can be assembled into shaders, but i see a lot of variables named chunks in this code. I was not referring to those big "chunks" just these things that the renderer appends or switches out. My assumption was that this code would remain in some form, i may be totally wrong about this.

This is what I meant by "You cannot really change an internal function in a GLSL chunk without rewriting it and including as another one. And it will be prone to break on every release".
GLSL chunks that WebGLRenderer uses are very inter-dependent and have large possibility to break if one is missed or used in incorrect order. Moreover, users struggle with hacks like `onBeforeCompile( replaceShaders )` to modify these chunks, increasing a possibility of breakage (for example, on each new release).
Nodes system doesn't have this problem.

I suggest we move this discussion to https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/16440. I think that issue also partially answers to your question why nodes system is located in Three.js repository -- because we want to have it in the core (ideally replacing shader chunks API 🙏) at some point.
author: LeviPesin

body: And I also think it makes more sense now to consider node system not as tool for working with and customizing materials, but as a tool for creating language-independent backend-independent code with high flexibility. This allows making node materials, GPGPU computations, et cetera...

body:
If we're talking about arbitrary three.js scenes, it might be hard to know whether a material has been configured with vertex tangents in mind, or what UV convention has been used (which would have related effects).

If we're talking about glTF source models, then it's helpful to know any `normalScale` specified by the model will be uniform: glTF does not allow different x/y parameters to normalScale. As a result I think you can safely assume:

- `Math.sign(x) === Math.sign(y)` material configured for derivative-based tangents
- `Math.sign(x) !== Math.sign(y)` material configured for vertex tangents

That is the problematic line:

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/00d426a28005aa4af49eaa0337bf40add6fa3545/src/renderers/WebGLRenderer.js#L985

Global clipping planes use the main camera (in this case `ArrayCamera`) whereas local clipping planes use the sub cameras since they are evaluated at a later point in `setProgram()`.

Looking at the code I think it might be possible to keep the `init()` call, but move to projection of the planes to `renderScene()` (so once per (sub)camera). body:
Your codesandbox passes a custom WebGL 1 context to the renderer. However, the shader only works with WebGL 2. Since we have slowly started to deprecate features for WebGL 1, we won't apply a refactoring here (eventually WebGL 1 support will be removed at some point). Just remove the `context` parameter from the `WebGLRenderer` ctor call and the shader compilation error goes away.

Besides, `toneMappingExposure` is no WebGL context parameter.

body:
There is already `BufferGeometry.computeVertexNormals()`. However, it's no good idea to add this call automatically to the `Mesh` constructor when a `MeshNormalMaterial` is used and no normals are defined. Simply because materials can be changed at a later point, too, e.g. from `MeshBasicMaterial` to `MeshNormalMaterial`. If you have no vertex normals, you run into the same issue again. The engine also generates no texture coordinates if you want to use textures but have no `uv` coordinates. 

Automatic generation of vertex data in such situations is not always possible and can lead to unexpected or unwanted behavior. It's really up to the user to generate these data if necessary. Or create the data upfront when designing the asset in a tool like Blender.

body:
The fallback to the root node is present since the new animation system was added via #6934. The specific commit which add `|| this.rootNode` in the constructor was  4985ed03dbfa59fb722af18fa66f4c347d034ae9. So this code was added on purpose. (BTW: The fallback is also used in `bind()` but it is not relevant for the above code example).

I' don't know if there is a use case that breaks if the fallback is removed and an error throw/logged instead. I'm not sure it's worth to find this out since you can easily use `PropertyBinding.findNode()` on app level to verify your track names and act accordingly.

body:

Could also argue the opposite: on app level, one could decide to use `PropertyBinding.findNode` and then assign the root node if that is really intended. I think I outlined above why I consider this a bug.

I ran into this case with files using `KHR_animation_pointer` which use way more complex property bindings (as seen here: #24108). 

Trying to sanitize each and every animation track before playing it on each and every asset doesn't sound like something that should happen on app level to me...

body:
Looking at the retarget function, I think the issue is related to keyframe generation, specifically the last keyframe. When the converted tracks are pushed, they currently push without the final keyframe. This is because the times and positions are assigned at every numFrame, but numFrame does not account for a final keyframe with no interpolation afterwards, only keyframes followed by interpolation. 

A solution could be: After the main loop, to add the final keyframe calculated a the exact `duration` timestamp.

I would love to work on a PR for this if it is open to work on.

body:
@timbotimbo this new PR should fix your issue. 

But in your example you will still have to change the way you get the fps from
`options.fps = 1 / result.clip.tracks[0].times[1];`
to
`options.fps = result.clip.tracks[ 0 ].times.length / result.clip.duration;`

Otherwise you're getting 
120.000047 fps
instead of 
120.20309 fps
this small difference is what's causing the issue in the amount of frames being short by 1.

But your issue highlighted other issues while debugging that should be adressed by that PR #27653

body:
For anyone who stumble on this problem, I found the root cause:

`expo-three` is importing `@expo/browser-polyfill` which changes the user agent to "chrome" => `navigator.userAgent = 'chrome'`.

Some libraries that target both the web and React Native check the user agent before using `HTMLElement`. In a normal React Native context the user agent is undefined and those libraries don't use any web specific classes. But once the user agent is changed to "chrome", the library will think it's running in a web environment (and in my case it will use `HTMLElement`).

Note : I was wrong to think that `HTMLElement` should exist in React Native.
author: obasille

body: Instead of introducing more configuration options, this use case can be handled differently without modifying the library. I suggest you create the WebGL context on app level and then pass it to the constructor of `WebGLRenderer`. In this way, you have full control over the context creation process and the respective error handling. This can be nicely encapsulated in a function `getContext()` on app level. And then you have just a single `renderer = new WebGLRenderer(...)` statement.

body:
Thought about this issue again and I think we should update the code in `WebGPUAttributes` to the version of https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/25295#issuecomment-1386776337.

It seems using `getMappedRange()` does only make sense if you don't have an array buffer in your app yet. Meaning you request one, create a typed array with it and then write your data into it.

In our case however data are already defined in buffer attributes. So it should be more performant to not create a new array and perform a copy but just use `GPUQueue.writeBuffer()`. The slow `.set( array )` part is then gone.

body:
Seems to be related to DoubleSide vs. TwoPassDoubleSide —

https://codesandbox.io/s/three-js-r148-opaque-to-transparent-forked-fb78yo?file=/src/canvas/ExampleBox.tsx
author: donmccurdy

body:
Normally, you have to set `needsUpdate` to `true` if you change the `side` or `transparent` properties after the first render of a material so defines like `DOUBLE_SIDED`, `FLIP_SIDED` or `OPAQUE` are correctly set in the shader. 

This was not necessary when the two pass rendering for `THREE.DoubleSide` was introduced since internally it was handled with two passes and `needsUpdate` was correctly set by the renderer. To be clear: It was not intended that setting `needsUpdate` to `true` on app level becomes obsolete. This was more of a side effect. Since `148`, this happens for `THREE.TwoPassDoubleSide` though.

So this is actually an issue in `@react-three/fiber`. The library should set `needsUpdate` to `true` when the properties are changed like in the codesandbox.

https://jsfiddle.net/auLnskfx/

body:
There is still multi material support in `ObjectLoader` as you can see here: https://jsfiddle.net/ndfg2aLr/

But it is required to maintain multiple materials in a plain array and not by using the `MultiMaterial` class. The class itself was deprecated in 2017 and the example code in the library was migrated to simple arrays.

The workaround for your case is to load the old JSON files with an outdated version of `three.js`, remove the usage of `MultiMaterial`, use arrays instead and then export to JSON again. These files should then load as expected.

body: Hm — more detail, I created that snippet by converting the file from .glb to .gltf with glTF Transform. Usually that wouldn't result in any other changes, but there are some serious validation errors on this file, so all bets might be off here. From https://github.khronos.org/glTF-Validator/, you can see a lot of errors with code `SCENE_NON_ROOT_NODE`. This means that the marked nodes are children of _both_ the scene, and of another node. Neither three.js nor the glTF specification allow nodes to have multiple parents, and it's likely to be the cause of the errors you see here.  That would indicate a bug in the software producing this file — the meshes may be reused, but the nodes must not.

body:
We made some changes related to scene graph loading that were intended as cleanup and optimization, but also likely fixed https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/18774, see more changes here:

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/pulls?q=is:pr+GLTFLoader+is:closed+milestone:r148+in:title

However, since a node is not supposed to have two parents, there isn't really any "compliant" way to resolve that situation in GLTFLoader, choosing between parent A and parent B. I think the result must have inadvertently changed here as a side effect of other changes.

body:
@Mugen87 I've implemented your idea, by holding two contour points arrays (left and right ones) and combining them at the end, giving the full stroke contour.

It works mostly correctly in the use case. I hoped it worked in the general case but you can see that the Tiger.svg has some artifacts. It is caused by the contour I generate, which folds over itself on some corners. Earcut does not like that on complex paths. As before, solving this would require checking self-intersections, but now in the linear contour, not in a triangle soup. It is doable, but only would work if the original path doesn't self-intersect.

Detail of the contour folding over itself:

![imagen](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/assets/2453652/66162363-7195-45bf-aa65-85fcfdf7b7d5)

Tiger.svg:

![imagen](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/assets/2453652/8869eee1-7a2c-4b26-9db2-502a3de710c9)

Use case:

![imagen](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/assets/2453652/52321dc3-f539-481c-ac1d-05fd9c3627f5)


Use case detail:

![imagen](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/assets/2453652/57909e7c-9550-4697-9a91-9dc8e92a0723)


The algorithm is smaller and cleaner (751 lines down to 495). Performance seems similar as before. The drawback is that the two contour arrays must be allocated. This can be optimized by providing reusable user arrays.

As the whole ```pointsToStrokeWithBuffers()``` is modified, I don't know very well how to integrate this change. For now I've just duplicated the function with the name ```pointsToStrokeContourWithBuffers()```.

The function ```pointsToStroke()``` accepts a boolean parameter ```useEarcut``` (default false) and selects which function to call.

It seems ```THREE``` doesn't export ```Earcut``` so I've copied ```Earcut.js``` in ```/jsm``` just for testing.

I've tried to make a live preview but failed. You can read the modified loader and example here: https://github.com/yomboprime/three.js/commit/563aea9172ada3e7dfa862e3125334415b40f336

Expecting your feedback to see if this can be useful.

body:

The problem is when the path points do auto-intersect like this (the contour gets flipped over itself):

body:
Hello, after several days of effort I completely rewrote the target function.
At first, I realized that the main problem was when two lines meet at a very sharp angle — their corner (miter) becomes extremely long and stretched. Our function was calculating this length, and once it exceeded a certain threshold, the line geometry broke apart.

This behavior is known as the miter limit, and it’s actually defined in the SVG standard to avoid exactly this issue.

I was able to fix the problem and fully repair the main function pointsToStrokeWithBuffers, so this issue is now completely resolved. You can see the full function in the link below.

Also, I want to say that I consider you, Mugen87, as my role model. I check your work every day, and I would be really happy if we could someday team up.

https://github.com/mahdi1212-max/Fixed-the-SVG-loading-issue-in-Three.js
author: mahdi1212-max

body:
> I wonder if there is a way to check if the bitmap was created with an imageOrientation

Not at the moment. `imageOrientation` is an options parameter of `createImageBitmap()` and the resulting instance of `ImageBitmap` does not report its respective options. If we want to evaluate it we need to store it right before this line:

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/4a6cf23c5f69ecde00e79171be7235deb39dff79/src/loaders/ImageBitmapLoader.js#L80

However, I'm not sure this approach works if two image bitmaps are loaded with different options in a sequence. Meaning:
```
loader.setOptions( options1 );
loader.load( ... ):
loader.setOptions( options2 );
loader.load( ... );
```
Depending on which image is loaded first, the wrong options might be stored in the image bitmap. So this would only be safe if `options` is a parameter of `load()`. But then the loader wouldn't have the common `Loader.load()` signature anymore.

body:
Related #22586.

Geometry generators just serialize their parameters, not their actual buffer data. When you do this:
```js
let newBufferGeometry = new THREE.BufferGeometry().copy( originalGeometry )
```
you copy the `parameters` objects which is used to detect geometry generators in `BufferGeometry.toJSON()`. If you delete it, the geometry is handled like a normal one.

The problem is the above copy is not valid right now. You have to copy the individual attributes, not the entire geometry. Otherwise you produce a property called `parameters` which normally does not exist in `BufferGeometry`.

The output of geometry generators can't be treated like normal instances of `BufferGeometry`. This is a deep conceptual issue and not easy to resolve.
author: Mugen87

body:
that makes sense, i was originally copying the individual attributes as you mention like so...
```
newBufferGeometry = new BufferGeometry()
newBufferGeometry.attributes = originalGeometry.attributes
newBufferGeometry.computeBoundingBox()
newBufferGeometry.groups = originalGeometry.groups
newBufferGeometry.index = originalGeometry.index
```
which works completely fine for the case in question but came across the error after @donmccurdy's suggestion to use the `.copy()` method

i'm way out of my league with this and there is of course good reason the `.copy()` method is written the way it is but could the following line in three.module.js...

```
// geometry generator parameters
if ( source.parameters !== undefined ) this.parameters = Object.assign( {}, source.parameters );
```
be updated with a needsUpdate condition like so...

```
if ( source.parameters !== undefined && !source.attributes.position.needsUpdate ) this.parameters = Object.assign( {}, source.parameters );
```
appologies in advance for any offence caused by what i think may be a rather naive suggestion, i'm most certainly failing to notice very important concepts of this method
author: forerunrun

body:
Um, I think the more clean solution would be to implement `copy()` in each geometry generator. In this way, the following problematic line in `BufferGeometry.toJSON()` could be removed.
```js
if ( source.parameters !== undefined ) this.parameters = Object.assign( {}, source.parameters );
```
Ideally, `copy()` methods do not copy properties of more concrete types. They should only work on data of their type.

However, implementing `copy()` in each generator produces some redundant code. But I guess it's better than what we have now.
author: Mugen87

body: Alternatively, geometry generators could be derived by a new base class `GeometryGenerator` that implements the new `copy()` once. Are you interested in giving this a try?

body:
The Angle Project runs a discussion group here, with tooling and people who can review browser environments.
https://groups.google.com/g/angleproject

Issues similar to this come up from time to time.  For example:
https://groups.google.com/g/angleproject/c/XMe7w989mUc?pli=1

To answer part of your question, javascript and webpages in general can not change the browsers settings or its operating environment.  Angle maintains a list of blacklisted hardware / driver / system update combos under D3D11 for the types of issues you are describing.

Why is it not default ?  It's a user configurable setting with a default configuration chosen by chrome based on OS, patch level, card type, driver version.  This configuration setting has the following description in chrome, enter this path in your browser search bar to review: chrome://flags/#use-angle

> Choose the graphics backend for ANGLE. D3D11 is used on most Windows computers by default. Using the OpenGL driver as the graphics backend may result in higher performance in some graphics-heavy applications, particularly on NVIDIA GPUs. It can increase battery and memory usage of video playback.

This library, three.js, only attaches to a canvas element within a webpage and requests a WebGL context.  At best, it can query for supported extensions in WebGL.  If the browser environment has issues, or issues with a particular extension, things fail.  If the extension is not supported by the hardware, things fail.  That's expected.  Not every example can run on all browser / hardware / OS / patch level combinations.  

The examples are written for clarity (most of the time) of a particular technique / technology.  They lack the feature detection and fall-back code that is added when people use this library.  They force a specific behaviour allowing people to see how things perform and catch issues like you have in specific setups.
author: epreston

body: Thanks @epreston but I'm aware that JS can't modify browser settings. My comment was specifically wondering about why OpenGL is not the default backend in ANGLE for Chrome as a browser setting rather than D3D11 since it seems to fix a number of issues which is why I mentioned the Chrome flag. I'm aware this is not a three.js decision to make.
author: gkjohnson

body:
I've reported the issue to chrome here:

https://bugs.chromium.org/p/chromium/issues/detail?id=1411048
author: gkjohnson

body:
It does not fix a number of issues, at best, it shifts the issues around.  For your current setup, it's a fix.  It has some benefits and drawbacks.

On some of the products I've worked with, D3D11 is used because the native windowing system uses D3D11 leading to a number of efficiencies, acceleration, and broader hardware support.  OpenGL code paths don't receive the same attention at each piece of the technology stack on Windows platforms.  There's no OpenGL certification requirement for Windows hardware, but there is a D3D11 validation requirement for signed drivers on Windows.

If you check out the purpose of the Angle project : https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ANGLE_(software)

It exists to translate OpenGL-ES to D3D11.  If they made OpenGL the default it effectively disables Angle.
author: epreston

body: Highly recommend raising this on Angle Project if you need a detailed / timely answer.  First thing they are going to ask is the graphics driver versions for your 2070 super and if you have the integrated graphics enabled on the i7.  Update the bug report or use the Angle troubleshooting tool dumps to reduce the cycle time on the request.

body:
Thanks for verifying!
Sorry, not familiar enough with three.js to give qualified feedback. I don't know whether multiple materials are (were) possible.
You've removed the index so the property is now `'.material.color'` which works in this simple case (and was the cause for this issue). I don't know if there are other cases where testing for type might be required.

Changing the colors parameters from "flat" to Object would probably break the interface and is not consistent with the other functions.
For convenience, add another function that supports this type or do type-checking?

Another thing to consider is that the property does not apply for other THREE Objects, e.g. lights don't have material but color. So maybe expose the binding string as additional parameter (with default)?
author: gnbl

body:
> You've removed the index so the property is now '.material.color' which works in this simple case (and was the cause for this issue). I don't know if there are other cases where testing for type might be required.

Multi-materials are supported but it's okay when the function does not remove it. `AnimationClipCreator` is intended for simple use cases. They should also give users a hint how animation clips can be generated procedurally. But they don't have to cover the entire feature set of `three.js`. That would only make them unnecessarily complicated and bloated.

> Another thing to consider is that the property does not apply for other THREE Objects, e.g. lights don't have material but color. So maybe expose the binding string as additional parameter (with default)?

The function is called `CreateMaterialColorAnimation()` so the relation to `Material.color` should be clear. I would not make the function more complicated by supported other color properties. Like mentioned above, `AnimationClipCreator` are focused on the most common use cases. If users need a different helper, they can easily copy one of the functions and update it according to their needs.

> Changing the colors parameters from "flat" to Object would probably break the interface and is not consistent with the other functions.

`AnimationClipCreator` should make the generation of common keyframe tracks easier. Looking at the other functions, using color objects should still be consistent.
author: Mugen87

body:
`Object3D.translateX( dist )` translates on the object's _local_ X-axis.

`BufferGeometry` has no need for such a method. `BufferGeometry.translate( x, y, z )` is sufficient.
author: WestLangley

body: I disagree, obviously. I would not have taken the time to submit this request if I thought it was ok APIs to be inconsistent within three.js.
author: philipswan

body: Having `translateX()`, `translateY()` and `translateZ()` in `Object3D` but not in `BufferGeometry` is appropriate because both classes have different semantics as https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/25368#issuecomment-1409518865 pointed out. So the APIs are not really comparable. Closing.

body:
When a regex expects indentation to be of a certain type like soft tabs, you can't change the formatting to hard tabs without updating the regex.

Or consider a regex expects code to be in a certain format like `doSomething(1,2,3)` something like `doSoemthing( 1, 2, 3 )` could also cause a breakage.

body:
Actually it is the primary purpose of the examples to demonstrate features of the library, not the documentation. All classes you have listed have a respective demo:

https://threejs.org/examples/#webgl_marchingcubes
https://threejs.org/examples/#webgl_geometry_nurbs
https://threejs.org/examples/#webgl_shaders_ocean

We have implemented a simple tags system some time ago, so you can search for certain terms like "water" and still find the `webgl_shaders_ocean` example. Or if you type "particles", you find "webgl_points_sprites", "webgl_points_dynamic" and many others.

So I could argue that you have just looked at the wrong place. Of course documenting everything would be better (like in every software project) but it is always a matter of time/budget to do this. And I'm not really a fan of stubs since they have to be maintained to certain degree as well and not necessarily create much value. TBH, I doubt it would help many users to discover features.

Besides, new exceptional features are always part of the release tweet by promoting them with screenshots.
author: Mugen87

body:
If you ask me, examples are examples, not documentation. 

In the case of the NURBSSurface, most of people I saw online in forum including myself have an hard time understanding the parameters. 
For me, three.js should hide a part of the complexity of the understanding of the NURBS surfaces for instance. If it is to eat the math behind it in the wikipedia page, it is better for me to reimplement it myself.

Examples are sometime sufficient to some degree to take a grasp of the implementation, but it will never replace the parameters documentation with the actual constraints. knots1, knots2 for instance are arrays with numbers in the example, but what is the actual meaning that I should deduce from the numbers the example is given. Without becoming a NURBS expert, three.js is not helping me much here.

If not putting stubs, I think we need to find a way to make it more discoverable. Scrolling through the examples praying to see something that ressembles to what we want to craft is a bit erratic to me. By seeing the actual stub not documented, it would attract maybe people that knows the field or want to interest to the field, to document it for people that have not the academic background, or that are in a rush of implementing it.

Again, I speak about my experience struggling to find anything. At the beginning I was assuming that the documentation was containing everything that composes three.js to wake up one morning discovering that it is not that close. 
I don't really know what we can make clearer in this context, it is why I wanted placeholder in the doc as a warning that there is much more than what is described in the documentation. 


EDIT: I wanted to add that most of the automatic documentation tool that I used in the past like compodoc to parse the JSDoc, are creating pages even if the file is not documented, I find it useful to also count what is the % of documentation coverage left to do. This type of metrics is kind of useful to have a feedback also on how much work is left to do.

I will happy to contribute to the documentation either if you respond favorably to this issue. I want to make this documentation a better place :)
author: AxiomeCG

body:
>Examples are sometime sufficient to some degree to take a grasp of the implementation, but it will never replace the parameters documentation with the actual constraints. knots1, knots2 for instance are arrays with numbers in the example, but what is the actual meaning that I should deduce from the numbers the example is given. Without becoming a NURBS expert, three.js is not helping me much here.

I'm afraid you are asking for something that the documentation will never fulfill. At least in `three.js` the documentation should document the API, not explain the concepts behind it. You essentially asking for exactly that which is clearly out of scope. 

For example the documentation for `CubicBezierCurve` states that the second and third parameter are control points. However, it does not explain what a control point actually is because somebody has to understand bezier curves first in oder to understand control points. But a technical documentation is not intended for supporting the dev in this context.

body:
I'm not really in phase with what you are saying.

For Spherical, the documentation states a number of things that could vary from an implementation to another.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/18304870/216023531-6ba59600-778e-4a84-b41b-283253eb7847.png)

Imagine the same without the documentation, with just an example. You cannot invent what choices have been made in the implementation for the parameters, you need some priors. And just showing off a spherical would not have been enough.

For sure I'm not asking for a full explanation about NURBS in the documentation. But what are the constraints of the parameters for example is too much to ask, you think ?

"This value goes from 0 to 1" / "angle's space is between -PI and PI", this type of documentation is tremendously helping if you ask me. And it is a pretty common thing to do in a documentation. You need to make clear what are the preconditions and post conditions of the API. 

For me three.js goal is to reduce the complexity through some abstraction. I'm maybe wrong about the assumptions I make.

At least I would have like a disclaimer of some kind on "How to abord the three.js documentation" stating that the documentation is incomplete and most of the magical stuffs are to be searched in the examples. It would have moderate my expectations. For the sake of the beginners, because it is not a usual way of abording a documentation if you ask me. (it is not a critic btw)

I was amazed and disappointed at the same time that this is actually the tip of the iceberg. There are many things I could have saved myself from implementing if I had had the presence of mind to understand it in the first place. (is it a whim to ask for clarification?)

I don't know how many people share this frustration, which is why I opened this issue. Now what can I do to help three.js improve on this point. I don't have enough background yet to understand each examples and document the addons accordingly to flesh it out.
author: AxiomeCG

body:
@0xAxiome it's not just you, the ThreeJS documentation is some of the most difficult docs I've ever had to work with, and reading through the examples can be cumbersome at best with the amount of (often questionably composed) boilerplate one needs to sift through in order to get to the parts of the (largely uncommented) relevant code.  That being said, there is often enough information in there to slowly piece together the information one is looking for, it just takes orders of magnitude more time than it would with good docs.

I just want to push back a little on this statement:
> We just want to avoid to put too much information into the docs.

[The BabylonJS docs page for curves](https://doc.babylonjs.com/features/featuresDeepDive/mesh/drawCurves) is a good example of the opposite side of the spectrum, with their examples used to _compliment_ sections of the docs with in an interactive playground as opposed to being used _as_ the docs.  Their actual docs are separate from their API docs, and imo, API docs can almost never substitute real docs.

It's one thing to say "we don't have the resources for comprehensive documentation", and another to say that the current state of the documentation is ideal because "the documentation should [only] document the API".  That's what typescript is for.  My IDE documents the API without the need to context switch, and it doesn't replace the need for real, thorough documentation / examples.

That said, the ThreeJS project, to my knowledge, doesn't have a very large, very well funded team working on it around the clock like Babylon does.  It's a rather small group of passionate open source devs with a much more limited scope.  I think it's important to point out that while the ThreeJS docs are _very_ far from ideal, they are certainly good enough to get by with some extra time and dedication, which is more than we can really ask for from such a small, dedicated team!

body:
I agree with you @FractalHQ on most of the points.

Moreover, I would want to really contribute to the documentation, but the guidelines about how to improve its discoverability is blurry to me. I'm really more about docs like the Babylon you shared. 

We can maybe find a middle ground. 

For me, each feature of three.js should have *at least* a WIP page with maybe the general sake of the module.
To make it discoverable, not just erratingly falling on by *luck*.

There is other problems to the documentation but it is not my point here in this issue.

But the time we think we spare not doing a "waterproof" documentation is the time we lose answering on discord channels, forums, stackoverflow... on very basic questions.

Really I would be happy to contribute, but we need a clear direction on where we want to bring the documentation. 

For me, it is not a good thing to keep it "on surface", throwing a bunch of examples and if the planets are aligned, you may or may not find something, an ugly js code that you should examine very carefully to determine what in the world was the developer thinking while writing it.

Just so you know, I spent hours on the NURBSCurve, found a way to make it work a bit. I'm still not able to tell what is a knot1, knot2. The person who wrote the code of the example must know it somehow. The examples by essence are not enough.
author: AxiomeCG

body:
> Their actual docs are separate from their API docs, and imo, API docs can almost never substitute real docs.

API references and tutorials are two distinct things, so let's not dismiss one or the other as not being "real docs" please. API references are a critical part of the three.js project, and we have to spend time on that (also see: i18n).

I understand you'd like three.js to have more long-form tutorials; so would I. Probably [three.js Fundamentals](https://threejs.org/manual/#en/fundamentals) or [three.js Journey](https://threejs-journey.com/) are more the format you're hoping for? Ultimately someone needs to write anything that is going to be added, but I think we're all broadly in favor of having more high-quality tutorials, in appropriate locations.

***

@Mugen87 I think I'm in favor of "stub" API references for things like THREE.Water and THREE.NURBS. Having links in the navigation going to a stub page does seem better to me than not having those links at all. And it may encourage others to contribute, if we request help on the stub pages. 😊

Those pages should remain API references, though. Any guides or tutorials can go in the appropriate sections.
author: donmccurdy

body:
It is unusual that critical infrastructure is undocumented. Having to dig through raw source code to figure out how to use something, why treat JSM/EXAMPLES in this manner?

1. Make it its own repository free from core
2. Add a peer dependency range for three so that it is always tied to a working core
3. Each primitive has a markdown with a templated structure explaining technical details and the API, and one or more simple but focussed examples that should not contain boilerplate — at least it should require both to be present for new PR's
5. (This would finally allow Threejs to focus on core matters and go semantic semver with breaking, feature and fix releases)

I would also suggest that the new addons repo opens up to more contributors that can review, lead PR's through and fix bugs. similar to how three-types and three-stdlib is handled.

body:
> It also appears any textures consumed in ShaderMaterial are in linear RGB, irregardless of the encoding property. Though I may be misunderstanding how this works.

Since the engine uses `SRGB8_ALPHA8` for sRGB encoded textures, WebGL takes care about color space conversion. The only point where a manual color space conversion is required is when writing to the default framebuffer (screen). Hence, you only have to add the `encodings_fragment` shader chunk to your fragment shader to fix the issue. 

I would have updated the fiddle but it seems the service is down right now. Essentially you need this line at the end of your fragment shader main function:
```
#include <encodings_fragment>
```
The problem is this code might not always be the final line of the fragment shader since other stuff (like premultiplied alpha) has to come later. And we don't know in advance how the custom shader looks like so it's not easy to document this. Besides, the above approach with `encodings_fragment` does not work for `RawShaderMaterial`s.

body:
**No, that change fixes this issue.** Thanks for the quick response.

As for your questions: I didn't find any specifics on what exactly triggers `pointercancel`, but we can reproduce it this way consistently. It probably happens when the two touches move so close that they "merge" into one, but that's just my guess.

> Besides, `pointerup` isn't fired in this scenario, right?

Yes. In fact, that is my current workaround: trigger a fake `pointerup` when getting `pointercancel`.

> I wonder if this issue can be fixed by simply adding state = STATE.NONE; in onPointerCancel()?

I'm not very familiar with the details of OrbitControls, but probably the other steps taken in `onPointerUp` (removing event listeners, dispatching end event) should be done too, except maybe the pointer capture release?
author: molnarm

body:
> I'm not very familiar with the details of OrbitControls, but probably the other steps taken in onPointerUp (removing event listeners, dispatching end event) should be done too, except maybe the pointer capture release?

Yes, that makes sense. We probably can remove the `onPointerCancel()` function and just register the `onPointerUp()` listener for the event. Meaning:
```js
scope.domElement.addEventListener( 'pointercancel', onPointerUp );
```

body:
Your expectation is reasonable, however, the mixer works in an optimized fashion that makes it not possible to properly support your use case.

The issue is the animation system compares any newly accumulated values against its own previously written cached values. The system would need to bypass its cache in order to detect modified values. This is not something we are going to support for performance reasons.

The policy is it's not allowed to externally mutate properties an active mixer owns.

author: eulertour

body:
The problem is that cloning a render target texture is an invalid operation. You have to clone the render target itself (the framebuffer) and not it's texture (the attachment). Besides, setting `needsUpdate` to `true` is also not valid for render target textures. Unlike normal (image based) textures, their data does not need to be uploaded to the GPU. 

The solution for this particular use case is to assign the texture value _after_ the uniform clone/merge operation. Meaning: https://jsfiddle.net/uc6ntLrw/
author: Mugen87

body:
Yeah, that was the fix I had settled on to work around the issue heh. I thought it would be a good idea to file an issue about it, since this feels a bit footgunny. I worry other developers could easily sink time tracking this down.

1. What's the expected behaviour of UniformsUtils.merge with normal image-based textures? Create a full on copy?
2. Since this is more of a helper function, would it be reasonable for it to check if they're rendertargettextures, dump out an error to console?
author: simondevyoutube

body:
> What's the expected behaviour of UniformsUtils.merge with normal image-based textures? Create a full on copy?

It should create a clone which means a new instance of `Texture` but with the same data source.

> Since this is more of a helper function, would it be reasonable for it to check if they're rendertargettextures, dump out an error to console?

Now that we have the `Texture.isRenderTargetTexture` flag, we could indeed implement a check in `cloneUniforms()`. Maybe it's a good idea of doing this.

body: Hey! Just noticed it seems this issue never got resolved. Have there been any updates on what should be done about max texture size or enhancing `options`?
author: mamcalli

body:
I can chime in on the reason: a common use case for USDZExporter is to use these files as-is for AR QuickLook on iOS devices. This is _very_ memory constrained, and 1024x1024 is a relatively safe choice for texture resolution so "medium sized" three scenes can still be converted _and_ run on older iPhones. 2048 and 4096 can work but have a higher risk of crashing these devices. 2-3 4k textures are pretty much guaranteed to crash on older iPhones, sometimes even just one.

We've also added a new `options` parameter to specify max resolution in our fork, so this can be a per-application choice when other limits can be verified for specific scenes.

body:
This is actually a problem on our side, it can be replicated simply with:
```js
import { Scene } from 'three'
export { Scene }
```

The tree-shaking issue was introduced in #24975, the `Object.defineProperties` is preventing WebGLRenderer from being tree-shaken when not in use, resulting in the additional 400 kB you see in the bundle.

I have a solution for this, will make a PR shortly.

body:
No, not yet. After #25599 we are still defining `dispose` as a class property.

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/8571a821c11762c179d20471db8f23a7ee847e60/src/renderers/WebGLRenderer.js#L574

To fix this issue we need to define `dispose` as a class method.
But we can't do that yet because we would need to use private class properties to avoid exposing internal WebGLRenderer stuff to the user.
author: marcofugaro

body: We can't use private class fields since we want to stick to ECMA2018. So if `WebGLRenderer` should really be refactored like suggested, private properties have to use the `_` prefix to mark them as private (see #25665).
author: Mugen87

body: Yup, should we refactor using `_` now or should we wait until we can use a new ECMA version in your opinion?
author: marcofugaro

body:
> Yup, should we refactor using _ now or should we wait until we can use a new ECMA version in your opinion?

We have lately decided to stick to ECMAScript 2018 (see #25185) and I would prefer to not revisit this topic in the next time.
author: Mugen87

body: @Mugen87 you misread my question. I asked if we should use `_` like you did in your PR (we can do this with ECMA2018), or wait until we can use a new ECMA version and use private class fields.
author: marcofugaro

body:
Sorry, you're right! We can use the `_` approach now and later replace it with private class properties. So we don't have to wait.

I'm just hesitating since refactoring big classes always introduces the risk of breaking something. But I guess sooner or later the mentioned inheritance issue with the renderer would come up again. My hope was to avoid the rewrite as long as possible^^.

body:
The issue is more specific than first thought.  It requires this specific sequence.

- ShaderMaterial added to scene object
- Render call
- copyTextureToTexture call
- Render call

If you move things so you can see it and don't delete first mesh before it's rendered, you can see that all textures are corrupted including the render of the ShaderMaterial.  Not just the two at the end.  The color shifts in an interesting pattern which points to the issue. 

Comment out the first render to see it work correctly.  We can now compare the correct state of the renderer to the state produced by the sequence above.

author: haeric

body:
> it's not comfortable and intuitive for end users

I'm afraid this is a subjective opinion. If you have all transformation options enabled at once, I could imagine frequent mistakes in the handling since users accidentally select the wrong gizmo. Or maybe just don't remember what gizmo controls which transformation. All of this does not happen with separate transformation modes. 

Besides, different modes allows you to visualize the gizmos in more specialized ways. I personally prefer the circle gizmos in the rotation mode than what the bounding box offers.

That said, I understand that in an _XR environment_ the presented bounding box approach can be advantage. Especially since you don't have to think about how the user toggles different modes which seems less of an issue with classic UIs and mouse/keyboard.

An ideal solution would be to make a new class that derives from `TransformControls`. That might require some refactoring in the base class though.
author: Mugen87

body:
To be clear, I'm not suggesting to replace the existing `TransformControls'.

I completely agree that there are cases where switching between `translate`, `rotate` and `scale` modes fits better.

> If you have all transformation options enabled at once, I could imagine frequent mistakes in the handling since users accidentally select the wrong gizmo.

That's why I wrote that it's not comfortable and intuitive for end users. I meant turning on all modes at once in the existing `TransformControls`.

This is exactly what the bounding box solves.

See how it's implemented in Babylon.js: https://doc.babylonjs.com/features/featuresDeepDive/mesh/gizmo

The position, scale, rotation and bounding box gizmos coexist together.

Moreover, it's possible to enable more than one (even all four) gizmo at once. I can imagine cases where this (e.g. enabling `translate` and `rotate` at the same time) might be useful.

> An ideal solution would be to make a new class that derives from `TransformControls`. That might require some refactoring in the base class though.

Another solution might be to add the new possible value `bounding-box` to existing `translate`, `rotate` and `scale`.

Allowing arrays for properties and methods related to modes will also be helpful.

body:
Assuming this is the same issue as the other thread this is a fairly odd situation the web as gotten itself in to where child iframes cannot properly identify native objects from other contexts. The suggestion from #24634 to render a small shader to see if it compiles is unreasonable, I think, but if we want to support that case you can differentiate context versions by just checking if a WebGL2-specific constant exists on the object:

```js
const isWebGL2 = 'SAMPLER_3D' in gl;
```

If the InteractiveGroup for the HTML Mesh isn't passed into the render loop, is there any method to manually update the HTML Mesh? Something like needsUpdate = true like with textures?

Since I'm not updating it often, I'd like to manually call update for new changes to the HTML.
author: user080975

body:
You can manually update the HTML mesh's internal texture like so:
```js
htmlMesh.material.map.update();
```
This will trigger the `html2canvas()` routine that redraws its canvas based on the latest DOM.

body:
Here is my template web page using a responsive page set currently to 1000 x 500:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/35147130/225012463-54cd7851-cd51-45c5-81a9-87e90182fcbf.png)

The layout is determined by bootstrap: essentially two columns one 3/12ths and one 9/12ths of the screen width. As I shrink the window size the columns will wrap like so:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/35147130/225012582-605fd3df-5d36-4e8a-9173-9238fb7b8eb0.png)

which is the form I want for mobile devices. All good so far. Let's focus on the first screenshot, where the canvas is 726 wide and 300 high. The renderer has been set using:

`      renderer.setSize( root.clientWidth, root.clientHeight );`

where root is the div element that contains the canvas. 

When I hit the "Enter VR" button there seems to be an implicit assumption that the render target is now the whole device and yet the renderer is not resized accordingly.  This is what my canvas looks like once I enter VR mode:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/35147130/225013058-9324b6b2-a924-469b-b402-e34c4e82226d.png)

Width is 1000 and height is 500 but the render size is still 726 x 300. By issuing a render.resize() before entering the xr session (since you certainly can't do one once the session is fully established) fixes this. Here it is about to be run in my custom XRButton code:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/35147130/225013255-57249177-6a31-40f0-9eef-f86d76597b15.png)

and here's the canvas after that resize is completed:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/35147130/225013340-b295c1ec-de34-4ed5-9228-e8020f3cdc33.png)

after which my sphere remains spherical once VR mode is fully established!

This is such a basic responsive design that I'm surprised this has not come up before.

author: AndyMGar

body:
I came across this thread because I was having a similar issue. For my WebXR app I was setting ```renderer.setSize(window.innerWidth, window.innerHeight);``` when setting up the renderer without realizing it's not needed for WebXR. When entering AR, my 3d objects would cut off at the bottom of the screen because the canvas size was set to the old innerHeight of the window before going fullscreen (device height minus browser bar).

For me, just removing ```renderer.setSize()``` resolves the issue because I don't need the browser/2d version but I can see how the renderer size is an issue when you want both. Another option was calling ```renderer.setSize(window.screen.width, window.screen.height);``` before setSession() is called.
author: ryanbarkernz

body: Thanks for the post Ryan. Yes just removing renderer.setSize would not have worked for me. I have not tried the other option as I'm currently working in 2D right now but will take a look when I return to 3D.
author: AndyMGar

body:
> The problem can be avoided, sort of, by passing updateStyle = false to the original renderer.setSize function. 

The `updateStyle = false` clue was actually the important bit. Sorry for overlooking it

What you see is a CSS scaling artifact of the canvas element. `updateStyle` controls whether `setSize()` writes `canvas.style.width/height;`. When using XR, the runtime matters. The distortion appears only with runtimes that present immersive content by scaling the page canvas itself — the WebXR emulator, inline sessions, and the WebXR polyfill. With `setSize(clientW, clientH, true)` the canvas carries an explicit, wide CSS box when the session scales that canvas to a differently-shaped display, the image is stretched non-uniformly.

That's why the mentioned workarounds help: `updateStyle = false` removes the explicit CSS box (but breaks the responsive layout since the canvas no longer tracks the container), and `setSize(innerWidth, innerHeight)` in `VRButton` makes the canvas aspect match the display so the scaling stays uniform.

On a native headset though the canvas isn't the presented surface, so the canvas CSS is irrelevant — which is why we couldn't reproduce it and why we won't add a `setSize()` into `VRButton`. It has simply no effect on native and the pre-session size is restored on sessionend.

Closing as can't-reproduce on native hardware / CSS-presentation artifact on canvas-presenting runtimes.

body:
If you would use the latest version of `three.js` (or at least `r148`), `GLTFExporter` would throw an exception in this case since exporters do not support textures with no image data , see #25106.

So before you export to glTF, you have to preprocess your textures and set all map properties to null when no image data are found. 

Even better would be to fix the FBX import since loading an asset with incomplete resources is an error in the first place.
author: Mugen87

body: Just saw the news, I changed the source code and solved it that day. Does the old version support it? Another new issue has been discovered: After exporting, even if a complete model is exported, the animation will be lost
author: calmheaded

body:
> Does the old version support it

Yes, the mentioned fix (setting the map properties to `null`) works with older versions of `three.js`, too.

> After exporting, even if a complete model is exported, the animation will be lost

Make sure to add the animation clips to the `options` parameter of `GLTFExporter.parse()`, see https://threejs.org/docs/index.html?q=gltfe#examples/en/exporters/GLTFExporter.parse

author: Mugen87

body:
@mrdoob I have started to implement `InstancedSprite` and encountered a problem.

I wanted to port [webgl_points_sprites](https://threejs.org/examples/webgl_points_sprites) to `InstancedSprite` but failed since the entire point cloud is rotated in the animation loop. Sprites however do not honor any changes applied to their `rotation` or `quaternion` property. There is `SpriteMaterial.rotation` for that: 

https://jsfiddle.net/vktsy0qr/1/

This is actually an issue since point clouds are frequently transformed via rotation. And since `InstancedSprite` is derived from `Sprite`, I wonder how the issue can be solved.

- Should `InstancedSprite` not support `Object3D.rotation`/`Object3D.quaternion` like `Sprite`?
-  If `InstancedSprite` should support rotation, it will still not be possible to rotate individual sprites via `instanceMatrix`. I guess we need a separate API `setRotationAt()` and `getRotationAt()` like with colors (probably with a separate instanced attribute).
author: Mugen87

body:
Interesting...

Probably better to not derive from `Sprite` then.

The idea of `InstancedSprite` was just to be able have something like `Points` with size support.

Maybe it would be better to try to implement `Points` using instancing internally instead?

Or maybe a new class with a different name?

body:
this.scene.clear()
this.scene.remove()
this.scene = null

上面 3 行代码仅仅是 清空了 scene 中的子项，子项仅仅是被移除而不是销毁。

虽然你后面贴出来了一个 modelDispose(objList) 函数，但是看不出你什么时候调用了该函数(到底执行了没不能确定)。

还有一点 modelDispose(objList) 里面的代码仅仅对 几何体和材质 进行了销毁，但是 纹理 并没有销毁。
(材质销毁不意味着纹理会跟着销毁)

------

_为什么214MB的模型会在加载过程中占用2G+大小？_

简单来说模型都是 “被压缩的数据”，加载到 three.js 后需要将数据解析，其中包含 “数据解压”、“数据类型转换”，自然所占内存要大于文件本身。

author: dbt-lucka

body:
Hey all,

I have managed to get a RoomPlan generated USDZ file loaded and rendering by patching the USDZLoader

This was just for a technical POC so it's very experimental, but it is possible! Good chance it breaks other stuff, and won't support all use cases, but for me I just wanted to render a `CapturedRoom` exported with the [model export option](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/roomplan/capturedroom/usdexportoptions/model)

I've attached the room I was loading - fairly basic & no furniture - [RoomModel.usdz.zip](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/files/13499387/RoomModel.usdz.zip)

Patch can be found in my [gist](https://gist.github.com/Matt-McConway/e13274531732fe25795115fc4bae3bb7)

Just a couple of changes were required

1. Change to the parser to check for array declaration 
    - The issue I was having was the `prepend references` field in some of my `.usda` files being an array of asset paths instead of a single asset path
2. Change to how geometries are built
    - Because there's now an array of assets, I merged them into a single `BufferGeometry` with the `mergeGeometries` util
    - I also added a check for finding the transform matrix, and apply it to the geometry being built
    - **NOTE** I doubt this change handles materials for each of these referenced assets, as the same material will be applied to the entire combined mesh


Hope this helps move things forward 😄

author: Mugen87
sunag

body:
> I just wonder if we should use SkinnedMesh instead of Skeleton since this is the object the user works usually on app level.

This is a good point to consider. I wonder what kind of use cases others have. Personally, my use case for retargeting is similar to the SEA3D example. My source is a Mixamo skeletal animation (without a mesh) and target is a skinned character mesh. So the most ergonomic combo for me is Skeleton source and SkinnedMesh or Skeleton target.

One benefit of the Skeleton type is if users are working with a SkinnedMesh, it's trivial for them to access the Skeleton via the `.skeleton` property whereas if they have a bare Skeleton animation, they'd need to make a dummy SkinnedMesh as shown in #25763. IMO it's not the most intuitive, until that PR I'd always thought a SkinnedMesh needs a "mesh" based on the name. Also, many of the SkeletonUtils functions use Skeleton inputs already.

That being said I need to take a deeper look at the retarget implementation. I don't fully understand all the `options` but I get a sense that some of them may rely on Object3D behavior, at least for the `target`. For instance, these lines where it uses `target.matrixWorld`

https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/a55464eacb39e9a781a91d7848eb7401621d1045/examples/jsm/utils/SkeletonUtils.js#L63-L79
author: mattrossman

body:
Notes as I try understanding the usage of the existing options for documentation and code cleanup.

Options for `retarget()`:

- `hip` - name of the hip bone in the `source` skeleton
- `names` - dictionary that maps from target bone names to source bone names
- `preserveHipPosition` - if enabled, preserves the Y component of the target hip bone position and zeros its X and Z component
  - I assume this was intended to be used alongside some root motion logic but I feel like not useful in its current state.
- `preserveMatrix` - ?
  - The implementation doesn't appear to be preserving matrix data, confused what this is supposed to do
- `preservePosition` - if enabled, preserves the original `.position` of target bones **except** the hip
  - Why this is useful? The only bone that I'd expect to have a `.position` track is the hip, the others only rotate.
- `useTargetMatrix` - if enabled, this will maintain the impact of `target.matrixWorld` when calculating target bone positions (defaults to disabled, in which case the inverse of `target.matrixWorld` will be applied)
  - Seems like the default is what you'd always want so that you can retarget even if your target isn't at the origin.


Options for `retargetClip()` (all options from `retarget()` are also valid here):

- `useFirstFramePosition` - if enabled, applies a negative offset to the target hip bone based on its retargeted position on the first frame. This effectively "centers" the retargeted animation at the origin.
  - Is this needed as an option? Seems like something that users could handle themselves.
- `fps` - determines rate at which animation will be sampled
- `names` - same purpose as the names option from `retarget()`
  - Note that here it defaults to an empty array, seems like this is supposed to be an empty object

In the Sea3D examples code:

- [webgl_loader_sea3d_bvh](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/r108/examples/webgl_loader_sea3d_bvh.html)
- [webgl_loader_sea3d_bvh_retarget](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/r108/examples/webgl_loader_sea3d_bvh_retarget.html)

I see usage of `hip`, `names`, and `preserveHipPosition` options from `retarget()` and `useFirstFramePosition` option from `retargetClip()`. The `preserveHipPosition` usage doesn't count since it's using the default of false, and although `useFirstFramePosition` is used here I suspect this is only useful for that particular SEA3D asset. I don't see usage of the other options.

In practice, I can get expected retargeting results using only `hip` and `names`. I'm inclined to remove the other options from retarget unless we have a clear use case for them.
author: mattrossman

body:
`preservePosition` -> It can be useful if the source and the target have a different anatomy or size, in which case it preserves the original position of the bones, which physically would be the most correct but as we have exceptions like the character Dalshin from Street Fighter for example who stretches his arms would be It is important to have this option of choice.

`useFirstFramePosition` -> this creates an offset for the position resetting it, this can be useful mainly when using mocap that were not treated, some occasions it is better that the delta position is done in programming than by animation, for these cases it can be useful.

Maybe this retarget with up-to-date example files can help in creating a full example.

[jsFiddle](https://jsfiddle.net/pa0vjt4b/)

They get scaled x100 when added to the scene, but retargeting scales them back down to a tiny size.
Applying the scale again after the retarget messes with the root motion of the animation.
author: timbotimbo

body:
@timbotimbo Thank you for the example! I had similar scaling difficulties when trying the soldier & xbot models. That would be a good litmus test for the retargeting implementation, to make sure it can work for those assets as expected. I have used retargeting on my own GLB characters and Mixamo animations in a different project successfully so there must be a way to get those working.

@sunag Ok, I see how `useFirstFramePosition` can be useful for untreated mocap data. So far I have been using Mixamo animations which are pretty clean. Maybe a different name for this option could more clearly communicate how it centers the animation. To me, this name sounds like it preserves the first frame position in the result, but it does the opposite.

I don't fully understand `preservePosition` yet. In your example, would Dalshin be the source animation or the target character? I understand basic retargeting to work like so:

- align world position of root bone (hips) only, from source to target
- align world orientation of all bones, from source to target

Therefore, I would expect the target character's proportions (defined by the positions of bones) to remain untouched by retargeting (i.e. "preserved") without needing to `.clone()` them as I see in the current implementation. Only the hip bone's position is affected. Similarly, if the target's bones change proportions (e.g. Dalshin stretching arms), that would work too since these non-hip bone positions aren't affected by retargeting?

If however, Dalshin is the source animation, then I could see how a way to opt-in to transferring positions of bones other than the hip is valuable. However, transferring world positions 1:1 isn't what I'd want, because this would overwrite my target character's body proportions. Instead I suppose I'd want to apply the position deltas from the source animation (e.g. deltas on Dalshin's arm positions). It sounds like that'd be a responsibility of `retargetClip` instead of `retarget`, since it could calculate those deltas.
author: mattrossman

body: Here is the result I get when retargeting that pirouette BVH animation to the Soldier model: https://jsfiddle.net/mattrossman/byhm96dp/1/ I get the same result with `preservePositions: true` in the retarget options. Most of the options I try don't seem to have an effect. One difficulty in debugging this sort of thing is that `THREE.SkeletonHelper` is a bit of a red herring. It only shows the position of bones, not the orientations. It would be easier to understand how it's behaving with a [visualization like this with the axes of each bone](https://twitter.com/sastuvel/status/1632413407528865798). Normally I'd add a `THREE.AxesHelper` to each bone myself, though with the dummy SkinnedMesh pattern from #25763 that's difficult because `.visible = false` to prevent an error, so my axes also get hidden. So, I have to use the `helper.skeleton = skeleton` hack instead. Here's a copy that shows the axes for the source and target bones: https://jsfiddle.net/mattrossman/byhm96dp/2/ You can see that the models use different bone orientations in their T-pose (the Soldier has the +Y axis pointing along the bone chain). Maybe this could contribute to the bones getting twisted up. I don't remember if this implementation retargets orientations relative to the bind pose or not. Not sure why their position and scale is being affected though. The BVH clip doesn't contain any scale tracks for other bones. Besides, I feel that by default retargeting shouldn't mess with scale or position of non-hip bones.

I have a few outstanding problems * Some of my models have a root bone (I want them in general) but there is no root animation preserved that I can see (e.g. for walking you move the root x/z values) * Playing a mixamo walk animation directly on the mixamo model works. Retargeting the animation clip to itself (!) makes things move, but the character is doubled over and not moving forward (no root motion). Before retargeted animation is applied: While playing retargeted animation: Clearly I am doing something wrong, I just thought I would check in to see if this thread had made any progress on new samples etc. before I keep investigating. I was trying to retarget to other characters with root bones, but when I retargeted it to itself I realized that was not even working, so I am ignoring the root bone issue for now. (Normally you want forward root motion on the root bone, making it easier to turn off later without messing up the rest of the animation etc.) Thanks!

body:
Hi! It appears that you're looking for help with a React Three Fiber project. We only handle bug reports and feature requests for the three.js library via GitHub issues. I would recommend using the R3F Discord Server for this question, instead:

https://pmnd.rs/discord
author: donmccurdy

body:
The legacy UMD/Global build in build/three.js and build/three.min.js is the most practical way we have to support the classic way, and maintaining it (and all of the usage patterns and support that involves) is a problem. This is why we've been moving toward ESM over the past few years. 

As far as I can see, a "plain JavaScript wrapper" would be the same thing as we are maintaining now, and trying to move away from. Anyone is certainly free to build three.js from source, targeting whatever module format you prefer. But it would do us no good to replace these builds with another version of the same thing.

If you or others would like to create a repository that wraps or repackages three.js in this way, I think that'd be great.
author: donmccurdy

body:
Such wrapper is very very simple. Just the following lines should be placed in <head\>:
```html
<script type="module">
import * as THREE from '../build/three.module.js';
window.THREE = THREE;
</script>
```

Just the same.

author: LeviPesin

body:
Related #11616.

The discussion in the linked issue has the following definition: _In three.js, shadows are the absence of light._

Lights with zero intensity illuminate nothing so they can't cast shadows. Besides, a material that receives shadows also must respond to the respective lighting.

`three.js` implements its shadow mapping based on these principals. You need to implement a custom solution for this special requirement. I suggest you use the [forum](https://discourse.threejs.org/) if you need help in this context.

author: elalish

body:
I'm not actually sure it's directly DRACO-related - my gut feel is that it's "some async work happens and under some conditions that changes the order of nodes" related. DRACO decoding may just be triggering the problem here.

I think one question is if "node order is deterministic" is a design goal for glTF in three.js. I asked in the glTF slack and the spec people as usual say that the glTF spec doesn't have an opinion on such mundane things as "defined order of nodes". cc @donmccurdy, would be curious what you think about that in this context
author: hybridherbst

body:
Personally I want the node order to be deterministic unless it requires a lot of complexity in the GLTFLoader because the deterministic order may help testing, debugging, import-export circular, and so on.

And I thought the GLTFLoader is designed to keep the deterministic node order (Update: the loader didn't guarantee the order before, but at some point we changed to guarantee the order), but am not really sure if we tested/reviewed well with DRACO. If the node order is not deterministic in a certain scenario with or without DRACO, personally I want to fix it (unless it requires complexity in the code).
author: takahirox

body: I vaguely recall @donmccurdy telling me at some point that GLTFLoader would order things pretty arbitrarily, hopefully he can chime in here. If you really need a file-based order, you can achieve it (I think) with the `associations` that are returned. It's quite common for implementations to mix up the nodes especially between import and export, so the rule is to not rely on it, hence the use of names.
author: elalish

body: Yeah, that's why I posed it as an issue/question here, to understand more what the general consensus is. Given that glTF doesn't have any tools for artists to specify rendering order of objects besides node order, I do understand that artists try to use this (as in the file above) for design choices. By now I've seen a lot of glass objects (think glass inside glass inside glass, like a fancy LED lightbulb) that only work because of specific node orders, so I'm curious to learn more.
author: hybridherbst

body: The object order is somewhat arbitrary (e.g. should a node's light come before or after its mesh?), but certainly intended to be deterministic and influenced by the order of node children in glTF. No question that is a design goal. Probably the Draco compression just introduces some additional timing complexity (decoding w/ Web Workers) that breaks determinism here, and we should investigate that.
author: donmccurdy

body:
It looks to me like what differs is not the order of nodes in the hierarchy, but the render order. Screen capture:

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1848368/233894069-576ea952-2e44-4035-a199-734a5bec6789.mov

Note the printer order on the RHS of the screen is the same each time, but the render changes. I tried again with a slightly modified version of the scene where pivots are centered in each geometry, and that fixes the issue as you might expect, though I understand that's not always an option.

@hybridherbst - are you seeing something else indicating a change in hierarchy? Or should we be looking for a different cause?
